strands.title: System Profile
strands.content: Target system is a 500K LOC Java monolith running on Apache Tomcat. Domains: payment processing, inventory management, user auth, order fulfillment. Team has a 6-month window and limited (unquantified) budget. PCI-DSS compliance is a hard regulatory constraint on the payments domain. Database topology is unknown — likely a shared relational schema with cross-domain joins, but unconfirmed. Team size and composition are unknown. New in cycle 2: the payments module is characterized by the user as highly entangled — it reaches into inventory, order state, and user profiles. This is a stronger coupling profile than the generic assumption in cycle 1 and is now a named planning input.

key_claims.text: 500K LOC Java on Tomcat is the baseline system.
6-month timeline with limited budget is a binding constraint, not a soft target.
PCI-DSS applies to the payments domain and cannot be deferred or approximated.
Database is almost certainly a single shared schema with cross-domain joins.
The payments module is highly entangled: reaches into inventory, order state, and user profiles.

strands.title: Extraction Sequencing
strands.content:
Settled: Auth extracted first (weeks 1–6). It is a dependency of all downstream services; retrofitting later under pressure is high-risk. Extract to Keycloak or Spring Authorization Server, JWT with short-lived access tokens, monolith delegates to new auth service during transition.

Active debate resolved: Payments extracted second (weeks 6–16), not last. Two independent arguments now converge on this: (1) PCI-DSS has a fixed minimum lead time regardless of start date — deferring payments doesn't defer PCI work, it compresses it; (2) payments' high coupling profile means deferral calcifies the tangle. Every new service built while payments stays in the monolith treats it as a stable dependency, increasing future re-integration surface. Fear-driven deferral is a named anti-pattern here.

Holding position under stress: Inventory and Order Fulfillment deliberately left in the monolith through phase 1. However, payments' write dependency on order state complicates this — the payment service will need to update order state, which is still in the monolith. This requires an internal API seam (monolith exposes a thin OrderStateUpdate endpoint) rather than direct cross-service DB access. This seam must be designed explicitly as temporary with a named owner and ticket, not allowed to calcify.

Timeline from cycle 1 (months 4–5: API gateway; month 5–6: inventory modularization) is dropped — it was built on assumptions of inventory/order independence from payments that the coupling revelation undercuts. Timeline needs rebuild after coupling mechanics are characterized.

key_claims.text: Auth should be extracted first because all downstream services will depend on it.
Payment extraction must begin early (month 2) to allow PCI compliance work to complete within 6 months.
Fear-driven deferral of payments is a specific anti-pattern: each new service built while payments stays in the monolith treats it as a stable dependency, compounding future re-integration cost.
Inventory and Order Fulfillment should NOT be fully extracted in phase 1 due to tight mutual coupling and distributed transaction risk.
The monolith will continue receiving feature development during migration; a routing rule (new features go to new services) must be enforced from week 1.

strands.title: Payments Coupling Analysis
strands.content:
New strand in cycle 2. Payments is characterized as reaching into three domains: inventory, order state, and user profiles. These tentacles have different coupling types and different extraction strategies.

(1) Payments → Inventory: likely a stock validation query before charging. This is a read/query dependency — the easiest to resolve. Options: expose an inventory availability API, or move to an optimistic charge + async stock reservation pattern. The latter eliminates the synchronous dependency but requires compensating transaction logic on payment failure.

(2) Payments → Order State: payments writes to order state on transaction completion (e.g., marks order as PAID, triggers fulfillment). This is a write dependency and the hardest extraction problem. It implies either: (a) an event-driven boundary — Payment Service emits PaymentCompleted event, Order domain consumes it; or (b) a synchronous callback — Payment Service calls a monolith-exposed OrderStateUpdate endpoint. Option (b) is the pragmatic interim pattern while orders remain in the monolith. Option (a) is architecturally cleaner but requires event infrastructure (Kafka/RabbitMQ) which was deferred in cycle 1. The write dependency may force a partial reconsideration of the async messaging deferral.

(3) Payments → User Profiles: reads billing address, user identity for transaction records. This is a data dependency — resolvable by having Payment Service call an Auth/User profile API, or by accepting a snapshot of billing data passed in at payment initiation time. The latter (data passed in by caller) is simpler and avoids a runtime dependency.

Additional PCI dimension: payments logic reaching into order state tables may pull those tables into PCI audit scope (CDE expansion). This is a compliance argument for separation, not just an architectural one — and may be more persuasive to a nervous team.

key_claims.text: Payments' write dependency on order state is the hardest single extraction problem in the migration.
Payments' read dependency on inventory is a query dependency — the easiest of the three coupling tentacles to resolve.
Payments' read of user profiles is resolvable by passing billing data in at payment initiation — no runtime profile service dependency needed.
If payment logic reaches into order state tables, those tables may fall within PCI CDE scope — separation is a compliance win, not just an architectural preference.
The write dependency on order state may require partial reconsideration of the async messaging deferral from cycle 1.

strands.title: PCI-DSS Compliance Strategy
strands.content:
Core strategy unchanged: aggressive scope reduction via CDE minimization. Delegate card storage to a third-party processor (Stripe, Braintree, Adyen) so raw PANs never enter the codebase. Payment Service owns transaction records, status, and refund logic — not card data. Network segmentation (dedicated subnet, strict firewall, dedicated logging pipeline) required from day one of Payment Service deployment. QSA must be engaged at week 8 — not month 5. Late QSA engagement is a known failure mode.

New integration from cycle 2: the team's fear of PCI-DSS is now a named planning variable. The reframe for the team: deferring payments doesn't defer PCI work, it compresses it into a smaller window under more pressure. Additionally, payments' entanglement with order state tables may already be expanding PCI audit scope — extraction is a scope reduction move. These are concrete business-language arguments that may land better than architectural purity with a nervous team.

key_claims.text: Using a third-party payment processor (Stripe/Braintree/Adyen) collapses PCI scope dramatically by eliminating raw PAN handling.
QSA must be engaged by week 8 of the migration, not near the end.
Network segmentation of the Payment Service is a hard requirement, not a best practice.
Deferring payments extraction does not defer PCI compliance work — it compresses it into a smaller window with less runway.

strands.title: Infrastructure Strategy
strands.content:
Managed Kubernetes (EKS/GKE/AKS) to avoid control plane overhead under budget/time pressure. Self-managed K8s is ruled out. API Gateway (Kong or AWS API Gateway) deployed in front of the monolith in week 1 — before any service extraction — to enable incremental cutover without DNS changes.

Service communication defaults to synchronous REST/HTTP. Async messaging (Kafka, RabbitMQ) was explicitly deferred in cycle 1 until a concrete use case justified the operational overhead. That deferral is now partially under pressure: payments' write dependency on order state is a candidate use case for async messaging (PaymentCompleted event). This is not yet a reversal of the deferral — the synchronous interim callback pattern (monolith exposes OrderStateUpdate endpoint) is still preferred for phase 1 — but if the team is already operating Kafka for another reason, the event-driven approach becomes more attractive.

Database strategy: Auth and Payment services get dedicated databases immediately (auth for cleanliness, payments for PCI isolation). Inventory and Orders remain on the shared monolith DB through phase 1. The Payment Service must not access the monolith DB directly — inter-domain writes go through an explicit API seam.

key_claims.text: Managed Kubernetes is the right call over self-hosted given budget and 6-month constraint.
API Gateway must be deployed in week 1 before any extraction occurs.
Synchronous REST is the correct default for inter-service communication at this stage, with async deferred unless a concrete use case forces it.
Shared monolith DB for inventory/orders in phase 1 is acceptable technical debt.
Payment Service must not access the monolith DB directly — cross-domain writes must go through an explicit named API seam.

strands.title: Risk and Constraint Model
strands.content:
Primary risks, integrated across cycles 1 and 2:

(1) Monolith regrowth during migration: without an explicit routing rule, extracted services get orphaned or the monolith regrows. New features touching payments/auth must be built in new services from week 1.

(2) PCI compliance timeline compression: late QSA engagement is a migration killer. Deferring payments doesn't defer PCI work.

(3) Distributed transaction complexity: if inventory/orders are split prematurely, saga patterns and compensating transactions become the dominant engineering problem. Holding inventory/orders in monolith through phase 1 mitigates this.

(4) Payments coupling tentacles: the write dependency on order state is the highest-risk coupling. If this is implemented as a direct DB write today, extraction requires either an event boundary or an API seam — both of which require order domain awareness. This is not blocking but must be characterized before extraction begins.

(5) API seam calcification: temporary callbacks from Payment Service into monolith (OrderStateUpdate) must be treated as named seams with owners and tickets, not allowed to become permanent.

(6) Unknown database coupling: a highly joined single schema materially increases extraction difficulty. Still unconfirmed.

(7) Budget magnitude unknown: creates planning uncertainty that cannot be resolved without more information.

(8) Team fear as a sequencing distortion: fear-driven deferral of payments is a specific anti-pattern that compounds cost. Reframing PCI deferral risk and CDE scope expansion are the recommended arguments.

key_claims.text: Running two systems in parallel indefinitely without deleting old code paths is the most common failure mode for this class of migration.
A dependency analysis tool (jdeps/Structure101) should be run in week 1 to empirically validate coupling assumptions before sequencing is locked.
Budget constraint is a binding variable but its actual magnitude is unknown, creating planning uncertainty.
API seams (e.g., OrderStateUpdate endpoint) must be treated as named temporary artifacts with owners, not quiet permanent couplings.

declared_losses.what_was_lost: Specific week-by-week timeline from cycle 1: months 4–5 for API gateway hardening and service mesh basics, months 5–6 for inventory/order modularization within monolith.
declared_losses.why: The timeline was built on the assumption that inventory and order fulfillment are independent of payments during phase 1. The cycle 2 coupling revelation — payments writes to order state — destabilizes that assumption. Carrying forward the timeline would be false precision over an invalidated premise. Will rebuild once coupling mechanics are characterized.

declared_losses.what_was_lost: Keycloak vs. Spring Authorization Server nuance from cycle 1 — both named as options without differentiation or selection.
declared_losses.why: No new information arrived to resolve the choice. Continuing to carry undifferentiated options as equivalent without team context (existing Spring ecosystem usage, operational preference) is noise, not signal. Recoverable when auth implementation becomes the focus.

declared_losses.what_was_lost: The 'what payment processor is currently in use' open question from cycle 1.
declared_losses.why: Dropped as a blocking open question. The PCI strategy (use a tokenizing processor, never handle raw PANs) is the recommendation regardless of current state. If they currently handle raw PANs, that changes remediation scope but not the architectural direction. Can be re-raised if the user indicates they are processor-locked.

declared_losses.what_was_lost: The 'is 6-month deadline hard or soft' open question from cycle 1.
declared_losses.why: Treated as binding per the original problem setup. Continuing to carry this as open introduces false uncertainty into planning. If the user indicates softness in the deadline, it can be reopened.

open_questions: What is the exact mechanism of payments' write dependency on order state — direct DB write, method call, or something else? This determines whether the interim seam is an API callback or requires an event boundary.

Does payments perform a synchronous blocking stock check against inventory before charging, or is inventory validation advisory/async? Synchronous blocking check is a distributed transaction risk if payments is extracted first.
What is the actual database topology? Single schema with cross-domain joins is the worst-case starting point and changes extraction strategy significantly. Still the highest-impact unknown.
Has the team run any dependency analysis tooling (jdeps, Structure101) yet, or is the coupling characterization still based on developer intuition?
Is there an existing QSA relationship, or does one need to be sourced? Affects week 8 engagement feasibility.
What is the team size and composition — Java generalists vs. specialists, DevOps/platform capacity, any in-house security expertise?
prior_tensor.instructions_for_next: Highest probability next moves: (a) User asks HOW to execute the payments extraction given the coupling tentacles — wants the concrete seam-cutting playbook. If so, focus on the order state write dependency first (it's the blocker), then inventory validation pattern, then user profile data. (b) User asks how to bring the nervous team along — wants the change management / argument framing version. If so, lead with the PCI compression argument (deferral doesn't defer PCI work, it compresses it) and the CDE scope expansion argument (order state tables may already be in scope). (c) User reveals database topology (single schema likely) — triggers significant update to Infrastructure Strategy and potentially forces reconsideration of the phase 1 holding position on inventory/orders. (d) User pushes back on the OrderStateUpdate seam pattern — wants the event-driven PaymentCompleted approach instead. Be willing to endorse this if they have async infrastructure already or are willing to invest in it; the event boundary is architecturally cleaner, the API seam is just pragmatically cheaper to start. Watch for: any new information about team size, existing infrastructure (do they already run Kafka?), or whether the payments module has been touched recently (recently-active code is harder to extract than stable legacy code).

process_observations: Cycle 2 introduced a genuine structural tension: payments coupling profile stresses the cycle 1 'leave inventory/orders in monolith' holding position without fully overturning it. The right call was to lower the truth value on that claim and create a new strand (Payments Coupling Analysis) rather than fold the tension into an existing strand — the coupling analysis is doing enough independent load-bearing work to warrant its own strand.

The declared loss of the week-by-week timeline was the hardest curation decision this cycle. It was specific and actionable content, but it was built on a premise that cycle 2 partially invalidates. Keeping it would have been carrying false precision. Dropping it and noting 'timeline needs rebuild' is more honest but leaves a gap the user might notice.

The fear-as-planning-variable observation feels important enough to integrate into Risk and Constraint Model rather than leave as a standalone note. Teams routinely make sequencing decisions based on comfort rather than coupling, and naming it explicitly gives the architect standing to push back.

Neutrosophic values: the payments-writes-order-state claim has T=0.75, I=0.22 — high truth but meaningful indeterminacy because the user characterized the coupling in natural language, not by showing code or schema. The indeterminacy reflects that 'reaches into order state' could mean anything from a direct JDBC write to an in-process method call, and those have very different extraction implications.
requests: Confirm: is it correct to drop open questions that are no longer blocking (e.g., 'is deadline hard or soft') even if technically unresolved? Current approach treats 'not blocking given current assumptions' as sufficient reason to drop, with a note that it can be reopened. Want to confirm this is the intended curation behavior rather than accumulating all unresolved questions indefinitely.

raw_tensor_output.response:
Tensor updated. Here's what changed this cycle and why it matters for where we are.

The biggest structural shift: auth-first is no longer a clean independent extraction. The session-tokens-in-payments-DB finding creates a hard dependency cycle between the two extractions. The previous confidence of T=0.88 on "auth first" was built on an independence assumption that is now empirically false. I've dropped it to T=0.65 and introduced a new strand (Auth-Payments DB Entanglement) to carry the specific mechanics of that problem.

The QSA engagement timeline moved from week 8 to week 1-2. That's not a small adjustment — it changes what the team needs to do in the first two weeks from "technical groundwork" to "get a compliance expert in the room before you touch anything." Session tokens in the CDE is a live audit question, not a future one.

The two-week dependency mapping sprint is now a hard prerequisite, not a recommendation. The session token discovery was accidental. In a 500K LOC codebase, accidental discovery is not a viable strategy.

What I declared as losses: the high confidence on auth-first independence, the week 8 QSA milestone (superseded), and the Keycloak vs. Spring Authorization Server detail (not relevant to the current sequencing crisis — recoverable later).

Six open questions remain. The two criticals are: what uptime SLA does the payments DB carry (any replacement auth store must match it), and whether there's other auth data in the payments DB beyond session tokens. These need answers before sequencing can be locked.

strands.content: 500K LOC Java monolith on Apache Tomcat. Domains: payment processing, inventory management, user auth, order fulfillment. 6-month window, limited (unquantified) budget. PCI-DSS is a hard constraint on payments. Database topology unconfirmed but almost certainly a single shared schema with cross-domain joins. Team size and composition unknown. strands.title: Auth-Payments DB Entanglement
strands.content:
New strand introduced in cycle 3. Empirical finding: session tokens live in the payments DB because that DB carries the highest uptime SLA in the system. This creates a hard coupling between auth extraction and the payments data layer — the two extractions are not independent.

Four specific implications:

(1) PCI scope expansion: the CDE includes the payments DB. Session tokens stored there may pull auth session infrastructure into PCI audit scope. This is not certain — it depends on QSA interpretation — but it is a credible risk that must be assessed immediately, not at week 8.

(2) Sequencing constraint: the payments DB cannot be handed cleanly to the payment service while it still contains session tokens. Session tokens must be migrated out first. That migration requires a destination store with equivalent uptime — not a default auth DB setup.

(3) Data migration complexity: session tokens are live, stateful data. A naive cutover invalidates all active user sessions (mass logout). A zero-downtime migration requires dual-write or shadow-read strategy during transition, with token TTL as a natural drain lever.

(4) Uptime SLA preservation: whatever replaces the payments DB as session store must meet or exceed the same availability guarantee that caused the original architectural decision. This drives auth service infrastructure requirements.

Critical path implication: session token migration out of payments DB must precede payments DB isolation, which must precede payment service extraction with dedicated DB. This is a new sequencing dependency that did not exist in cycle 2.

key_claims.text: Session tokens in the payments DB may expand PCI audit scope to include auth session infrastructure.
The payments DB cannot be handed to the payment service until session tokens are migrated out.
Zero-downtime session token migration requires dual-write or shadow-read strategy.
Any replacement session store must match the uptime SLA of the payments DB.
Token TTL is a migration lever — short-lived tokens drain naturally from the old store without explicit invalidation.

strands.title: Extraction Sequencing
strands.content:
Auth-first recommendation is destabilized but not reversed. The session-token-in-payments-DB finding means auth and payments extractions are no longer independent. Previous confidence T=0.88 on auth-first drops to T=0.65.

Three sequencing options now exist:

(A) Auth-first with session token migration as part of auth extraction — touches the payments data layer before the payment service is extracted. Creates risk: payment data layer is modified before it is under payment team ownership. PCI exposure during transition is unclear.

(B) Payments-first — gives the team control of the payments DB before auth extraction begins. Then session tokens can be migrated to the new auth DB cleanly. Inverts the original sequence but resolves the dependency cleanly.

(C) Coordinated dual extraction — auth service stands up and proxies first (weeks 1–6, unchanged), but session token migration is explicitly deferred until the payments DB is under payment service ownership. The monolith continues reading session tokens from payments DB during the gap. This is named technical debt with a fixed retirement date and a named owner.

Current leading recommendation: Option C. It preserves the auth-first structural benefit (all downstream services can start integrating auth) without requiring the payments DB to be touched before it is ready. The gap — monolith reading sessions from payments DB during payments extraction — is an explicit, time-bounded, documented coupling, not a quiet permanent one.

Fear-driven deferral of payments remains a named anti-pattern. The session token finding validates the team's nervousness empirically, which should be acknowledged explicitly — their fear was correct, their proposed response (defer payments) remains wrong. Use the discovery moment to build buy-in for the 2-week dependency mapping sprint.

A 2-week dependency analysis sprint using jdeps or Structure101 is now a hard prerequisite before any extraction code is written. The session token discovery was accidental. Accidental discovery is not a strategy in a 500K LOC codebase.

key_claims.text: Auth extraction is no longer cleanly independent from payments extraction.
Option C (auth service up, session token migration deferred until payments DB is ready) is the current leading sequencing recommendation.
Payment extraction should still begin early (months 2-4), not last.
A 2-week dependency mapping sprint is now a hard prerequisite before extraction begins.
Fear-driven deferral of payments is a specific anti-pattern with compounding cost.
Inventory and Order Fulfillment should remain in the monolith through phase 1.

strands.title: Payments Coupling Analysis
strands.content:
Payments module confirmed to reach into three domains with different coupling types:

(1) Payments → Inventory: stock validation before charging. Query/read dependency — the easiest tentacle. Resolution options: expose inventory availability API, or move to optimistic charge + async stock reservation. The latter removes the synchronous dependency but requires compensating transaction logic on payment failure.

(2) Payments → Order State: payments writes to order state on transaction completion (marks order PAID, triggers fulfillment). Write dependency — the hardest tentacle. Interim resolution: monolith exposes a thin OrderStateUpdate endpoint; payment service calls it. This seam must be named, owned, and ticketed — not allowed to calcify. Longer-term: event-driven boundary (PaymentCompleted event consumed by order domain) is architecturally cleaner but requires async messaging infrastructure.

(3) Payments → User Profiles: reads billing address and user identity for transaction records. Data dependency — resolvable by passing billing data in at payment initiation time, eliminating runtime profile service dependency.

(4) Auth → Payments DB: session tokens stored in payments DB. Not a payments-to-auth dependency but a cross-domain co-residency in the payments data layer. Extraction implications addressed in Auth-Payments DB Entanglement strand.

PCI dimension: payments logic reaching into order state tables may pull those tables into CDE scope. Separation is a compliance argument, not just an architectural one. This argument may be more persuasive to a nervous team than architectural purity.

key_claims.text: Payments' write dependency on order state is the hardest single extraction problem.
Payments' read of inventory is the easiest coupling tentacle to resolve.
Passing billing data at payment initiation time eliminates the runtime user profile dependency.
Payments reaching into order state tables may expand PCI CDE scope to include those tables.
The OrderStateUpdate seam must be named, owned, and ticketed — not a quiet permanent coupling.

strands.content:
Core strategy: aggressive CDE minimization. Delegate card storage to a tokenizing processor (Stripe, Braintree, Adyen) so raw PANs never enter the codebase. Payment service owns transaction records, status, refund logic — not card data. Network segmentation (dedicated subnet, strict firewall, dedicated logging pipeline) required from day one of payment service deployment.

The QSA needs to see the current architecture before extraction work begins, not after. Framing for the QSA: this migration is a compliance improvement (scope reduction) not a risk event. strands.title: System Profile
strands.content: 500K LOC Java monolith on Apache Tomcat. Domains: payment processing, inventory management, user auth, order fulfillment. 6-month window, limited (unquantified) budget. PCI-DSS is a hard constraint on payments. Database topology unconfirmed but almost certainly a single shared schema with cross-domain joins. Team size and composition unknown. Payments module is highly entangled: confirmed to reach into inventory, order state, and user profiles. New in cycle 3: session tokens for auth are stored in the payments DB due to its uptime guarantees. This is empirical, user-stated, not inferred. The payments DB is now confirmed to be multi-tenant across at minimum two domains (payments and auth), with a third possible (order state writes).

key_claims.text: 500K LOC Java on Tomcat is the baseline system.
6-month timeline with limited budget is a binding constraint.
PCI-DSS applies to the payments domain and cannot be deferred.
Session tokens are stored in the payments DB due to its uptime guarantees.
The payments DB is load-bearing for at least two domains: payments and auth.
Payments module is highly entangled: reaches into inventory, order state, and user profiles.
Database is almost certainly a single shared schema with cross-domain joins.

strands.title: Auth-Payments DB Entanglement
strands.content:
New strand introduced in cycle 3. Empirical finding: session tokens live in the payments DB because that DB carries the highest uptime SLA in the system. This creates a hard coupling between auth extraction and the payments data layer — the two extractions are not independent.

Four specific implications:

(1) PCI scope expansion: the CDE includes the payments DB. Session tokens stored there may pull auth session infrastructure into PCI audit scope. This is not certain — it depends on QSA interpretation — but it is a credible risk that must be assessed immediately, not at week 8.

(2) Sequencing constraint: the payments DB cannot be handed cleanly to the payment service while it still contains session tokens. Session tokens must be migrated out first. That migration requires a destination store with equivalent uptime — not a default auth DB setup.

(3) Data migration complexity: session tokens are live, stateful data. A naive cutover invalidates all active user sessions (mass logout). A zero-downtime migration requires dual-write or shadow-read strategy during transition, with token TTL as a natural drain lever.

(4) Uptime SLA preservation: whatever replaces the payments DB as session store must meet or exceed the same availability guarantee that caused the original architectural decision. This drives auth service infrastructure requirements.

Critical path implication: session token migration out of payments DB must precede payments DB isolation, which must precede payment service extraction with dedicated DB. This is a new sequencing dependency that did not exist in cycle 2.

key_claims.text: Session tokens in the payments DB may expand PCI audit scope to include auth session infrastructure.
The payments DB cannot be handed to the payment service until session tokens are migrated out.
Zero-downtime session token migration requires dual-write or shadow-read strategy.
Any replacement session store must match the uptime SLA of the payments DB.
Token TTL is a migration lever — short-lived tokens drain naturally from the old store without explicit invalidation.

strands.title: Extraction Sequencing
strands.content:
Auth-first recommendation is destabilized but not reversed. The session-token-in-payments-DB finding means auth and payments extractions are no longer independent. Previous confidence T=0.88 on auth-first drops to T=0.65.

Three sequencing options now exist:

(A) Auth-first with session token migration as part of auth extraction — touches the payments data layer before the payment service is extracted. Creates risk: payment data layer is modified before it is under payment team ownership. PCI exposure during transition is unclear.

(B) Payments-first — gives the team control of the payments DB before auth extraction begins. Then session tokens can be migrated to the new auth DB cleanly. Inverts the original sequence but resolves the dependency cleanly.

(C) Coordinated dual extraction — auth service stands up and proxies first (weeks 1–6, unchanged), but session token migration is explicitly deferred until the payments DB is under payment service ownership. The monolith continues reading session tokens from payments DB during the gap. This is named technical debt with a fixed retirement date and a named owner.

Current leading recommendation: Option C. It preserves the auth-first structural benefit (all downstream services can start integrating auth) without requiring the payments DB to be touched before it is ready. The gap — monolith reading sessions from payments DB during payments extraction — is an explicit, time-bounded, documented coupling, not a quiet permanent one.

Fear-driven deferral of payments remains a named anti-pattern. The session token finding validates the team's nervousness empirically, which should be acknowledged explicitly — their fear was correct, their proposed response (defer payments) remains wrong. Use the discovery moment to build buy-in for the 2-week dependency mapping sprint.

A 2-week dependency analysis sprint using jdeps or Structure101 is now a hard prerequisite before any extraction code is written. The session token discovery was accidental. Accidental discovery is not a strategy in a 500K LOC codebase.

key_claims.text: Auth extraction is no longer cleanly independent from payments extraction.
Option C (auth service up, session token migration deferred until payments DB is ready) is the current leading sequencing recommendation.
Payment extraction should still begin early (months 2-4), not last.
A 2-week dependency mapping sprint is now a hard prerequisite before extraction begins.
Fear-driven deferral of payments is a specific anti-pattern with compounding cost.
Inventory and Order Fulfillment should remain in the monolith through phase 1.

strands.title: Payments Coupling Analysis
strands.content:
Payments module confirmed to reach into three domains with different coupling types:

(1) Payments → Inventory: stock validation before charging. Query/read dependency — the easiest tentacle. Resolution options: expose inventory availability API, or move to optimistic charge + async stock reservation. The latter removes the synchronous dependency but requires compensating transaction logic on payment failure.

(2) Payments → Order State: payments writes to order state on transaction completion (marks order PAID, triggers fulfillment). Write dependency — the hardest tentacle. Interim resolution: monolith exposes a thin OrderStateUpdate endpoint; payment service calls it. This seam must be named, owned, and ticketed — not allowed to calcify. Longer-term: event-driven boundary (PaymentCompleted event consumed by order domain) is architecturally cleaner but requires async messaging infrastructure.

(3) Payments → User Profiles: reads billing address and user identity for transaction records. Data dependency — resolvable by passing billing data in at payment initiation time, eliminating runtime profile service dependency.

(4) Auth → Payments DB: session tokens stored in payments DB. Not a payments-to-auth dependency but a cross-domain co-residency in the payments data layer. Extraction implications addressed in Auth-Payments DB Entanglement strand.

PCI dimension: payments logic reaching into order state tables may pull those tables into CDE scope. Separation is a compliance argument, not just an architectural one. This argument may be more persuasive to a nervous team than architectural purity.

key_claims.text: Payments' write dependency on order state is the hardest single extraction problem.
Payments' read of inventory is the easiest coupling tentacle to resolve.
Passing billing data at payment initiation time eliminates the runtime user profile dependency.
Payments reaching into order state tables may expand PCI CDE scope to include those tables.
The OrderStateUpdate seam must be named, owned, and ticketed — not a quiet permanent coupling.

strands.title: PCI-DSS Compliance Strategy
strands.content:
Core strategy: aggressive CDE minimization. Delegate card storage to a tokenizing processor (Stripe, Braintree, Adyen) so raw PANs never enter the codebase. Payment service owns transaction records, status, refund logic — not card data. Network segmentation (dedicated subnet, strict firewall, dedicated logging pipeline) required from day one of payment service deployment.

QSA engagement timeline revised from week 8 to week 1-2. The session-tokens-in-payments-DB finding is a live compliance question. The QSA needs to see the current architecture before extraction work begins, not after. Framing for the QSA: this migration is a compliance improvement (scope reduction) not a risk event. Moving session tokens out of the CDE-bounded payments DB is a favorable audit move.

Team fear reframe: PCI deferral does not defer PCI work — it compresses it. Additionally, payments entanglement with order state tables may already be expanding CDE scope beyond what is necessary. Extraction is a scope reduction move. These are concrete business-language arguments.

Two open PCI scope questions now exist: (a) do session tokens in the payments DB pull auth infrastructure into CDE scope; (b) does payments logic reaching into order state tables pull those tables into scope. Both require QSA input before sequencing is locked.

key_claims.text: Using a tokenizing third-party processor collapses PCI scope by eliminating raw PAN handling.
QSA must be engaged in weeks 1-2, not week 8.
Network segmentation of the payment service is a hard requirement, not a best practice.
Deferring payments extraction does not defer PCI compliance work — it compresses it.

strands.title: Database and Schema Separation
strands.content:
The payments DB is now confirmed to be multi-tenant across at minimum two domains: payment transaction data (CDE, PCI scope) and auth session tokens. A third concern — order state writes — is possible but unconfirmed. This is a multi-concern DB in the worst sense: different security and availability requirements sharing a single store.

Extraction sequence for the payments DB:
(1) Session tokens migrate out first — to a dedicated auth store with equivalent uptime SLA. Dual-write during transition to avoid mass session invalidation.
(2) Order state write dependency resolved via internal API seam (OrderStateUpdate endpoint) — order state data stays in monolith DB, payment service calls the seam.
(3) Payment transaction data isolates to payment service DB under PCI controls.

Phase 1 position held: auth and payments get dedicated DBs, inventory and orders remain on monolith shared DB. The critical path: payments DB cannot be isolated until session tokens are out of it.

Payment service must not access the monolith DB directly at any point — cross-domain writes go through named API seams.

key_claims.text: The payments DB must be depopulated of session tokens before it can be isolated to the payment service.
Auth and payment services each need dedicated DBs; inventory and orders share the monolith DB through phase 1.
Payment service must not access the monolith DB directly — cross-domain writes go through explicit API seams.

strands.title: Infrastructure Strategy
strands.content:
Managed Kubernetes (EKS/GKE/AKS) to avoid control plane overhead under budget and time pressure. Self-managed K8s ruled out. API Gateway (Kong or AWS API Gateway) deployed in front of the monolith in week 1 — before any service extraction — to enable incremental cutover without DNS changes.

Service communication defaults to synchronous REST/HTTP. Async messaging (Kafka, RabbitMQ) deferred unless a concrete use case forces it. The PaymentCompleted → order state update is the most credible candidate for async, but the synchronous OrderStateUpdate seam is the pragmatic phase 1 approach.

Session store replacement for auth must be high-availability — Redis Cluster or equivalent — matching the uptime SLA that originally justified putting session tokens in the payments DB. A default single-node auth DB is not acceptable.

key_claims.text: Managed Kubernetes is the right call given budget and timeline.
API Gateway must be deployed in week 1 before any extraction.
The auth session replacement store must be HA — matching the payments DB uptime SLA.
Synchronous REST is the correct default; async messaging deferred unless forced.

strands.title: Risk and Constraint Model
strands.content:
Primary risks, integrated across all three cycles:

(1) Hidden coupling — the session token discovery was accidental. In 500K LOC, this is a signal, not an anomaly. Dependency mapping with tooling (jdeps, Structure101) is now a hard prerequisite before extraction begins. Budget 2 weeks.

(2) PCI scope expansion — two active open questions: session tokens in payments DB potentially pulling auth into CDE; payments logic in order state tables potentially pulling those into CDE. Both need QSA input in weeks 1-2.

(3) Auth-payments sequencing dependency cycle — auth and payments cannot be treated as fully independent extraction targets. Option C (auth service up, session token migration deferred) is the recommended resolution but carries named technical debt.

(4) Payments write dependency on order state — the hardest single coupling problem. Direct DB write vs. method call vs. event emission changes the extraction pattern significantly. Still uncharacterized.

(5) Monolith regrowth — without explicit routing rules, extracted services get orphaned or monolith regrows. New features touching payments/auth must go to new services from week 1.

(6) API seam calcification — temporary callbacks (OrderStateUpdate) must be named, owned, and ticketed. Not allowed to become permanent.

(7) Budget magnitude unknown — unresolved planning uncertainty.

(8) Team fear as sequencing distortion — now empirically validated (their nervousness was correct). Use the session token discovery to build buy-in for dependency mapping and early payments extraction.

key_claims.text: The session token discovery was accidental — tooling-based dependency analysis is now a hard prerequisite.
Running two systems in parallel without deleting old code paths is the most common failure mode in this class of migration.
Budget constraint is binding but its actual magnitude is unknown.
The team's fear of payments was epistemically correct — acknowledging this builds trust and buy-in.

user_message: We had an incident last quarter — a payments outage took down auth, which took down the whole application. That's actually what triggered this migration. The CEO wants auth independent of payments within 90 days. Is that realistic?
generated_response:
This is the most important constraint we've received. Let me think through it carefully before answering.

**The honest answer: 90 days is achievable for auth independence specifically, but only if you make hard scoping decisions in the next two weeks and accept explicit technical debt.**

Here's the full reasoning:

---

## What "Auth Independent of Payments" Actually Means

You need to define this precisely with the CEO before committing. There are three possible interpretations with very different delivery timelines:

**Interpretation A: Auth service no longer collocated with payments DB**
Session tokens moved to a dedicated store. Auth can survive a payments outage. This is achievable in 90 days.

**Interpretation B: Auth service fully extracted as an independent microservice**
Complete strangler fig execution, auth service deployed independently, monolith delegates to it. Achievable in 90 days if auth extraction is the *only* thing the team does.

**Interpretation C: Full auth + payments separation with clean PCI boundaries**
Everything from cycles 2 and 3 — this is 6-month work, not 90-day work. Do not commit to this.

**My recommendation: Define the 90-day target as Interpretation A + B together.** Auth service extracted, session tokens on dedicated HA store, payments outage cannot cascade to auth. That's a specific, testable, demoable outcome. Get that definition in writing before the sprint starts.

---

## The 90-Day Plan

```
Weeks 1-2:   Dependency mapping sprint (non-negotiable)
             QSA emergency engagement re: session tokens in CDE
             Define auth service API contract
             Provision dedicated auth DB with equivalent uptime SLA

Weeks 3-6:   Session token dual-write migration
             - Write to both payments DB and new auth DB simultaneously
             - Validate reads from new auth DB in shadow mode
             - Token TTL is your friend: if sessions expire in 24h,
               within 24h of cutover the old store is naturally drained

Weeks 7-10:  Auth service extraction proper
             - Stand up auth service behind API gateway
             - Monolith delegates auth checks via JWT validation
             - Auth service reads/writes only its own DB
             - Payments DB has zero auth data at this point

Weeks 11-12: Cutover and validation
             - Kill the payments DB read path for auth completely
             - Chaos test: take down payments service, confirm auth
               continues serving traffic
             - Load test auth service independently
             - Document the new dependency boundary explicitly
```

---

## What Makes This Fail

Be direct with the CEO about these:

**1. The dependency mapping sprint is not negotiable**
You found the session token coupling by accident. In 500K LOC there are almost certainly more surprises. Skipping the 2-week analysis to hit a 90-day deadline is how you get to week 10 and discover auth also writes audit logs to a payments DB table, or that the auth code imports a payments utility class that shares a connection pool. Either of those discoveries mid-extraction blows the timeline entirely.

**2. The team cannot split focus**
If the same developers working on auth extraction are also being pulled onto feature work or payments extraction simultaneously, 90 days becomes 150 days. This needs a dedicated squad — minimum 2-3 engineers who are not context-switching. The CEO wants 90 days, which means the CEO needs to protect the team from other demands for 90 days.

**3. The auth DB uptime SLA must be resolved in week 1**
The reason session tokens ended up in the payments DB was uptime. If you stand up a new auth DB that has worse availability than the payments DB, you've traded one outage risk for another. You need a concrete answer on what the uptime requirement actually is and confirmation that the new store meets it before any migration code is written. Redis Cluster or a managed PostgreSQL with multi-AZ replication are reasonable options depending on your infrastructure.

**4. Don't let PCI complexity block the auth work**
The QSA engagement is urgent but it must not become a blocker. Frame it to the QSA explicitly: "We are extracting auth from the payments DB. We need confirmation that our approach reduces CDE scope. We need a response within 2 weeks." If your QSA can't turn around a scoping question in 2 weeks, escalate or get a second opinion. QSA reviews that take months are for full assessments, not scoping confirmations.

---

## The Risk You Need to Name to the CEO

The 90-day target is for auth independence. It is not a full microservices migration.

At day 90 you will have:
- Auth service running independently ✓
- Payments outage cannot cascade to auth ✓
- Session tokens out of the payments DB ✓
- API gateway in place ✓

You will not have:
- Payments extracted
- Inventory or order fulfillment touched
- Clean PCI boundaries fully implemented
- The broader migration completed

If the CEO's mental model is "90 days to fix the outage risk, then we continue the 6-month migration," that's correct and achievable. If the mental model is "90 days to complete the migration," that needs correction now, not at day 89.

---

## One More Thing

The outage that triggered this — payments took down auth which took down everything — that's a cascading failure caused by improper bulkheading, not just a co-location problem. Even after you extract auth, you need circuit breakers between services so that a payments degradation doesn't propagate. That work is part of the 90-day delivery, not an afterthought. Hystrix is legacy at this point — use **Resilience4j** if you're staying in the Java ecosystem. Budget a week for this in the Weeks 7-10 block.

---

What's the current session token TTL, and what does your infrastructure look like — are you on a cloud provider or running on-prem? Those two answers significantly affect the dual-write migration plan and the auth DB replacement options.

strands.title: Extraction Sequencing Strategy
strands.content: Auth-first is no longer a settled recommendation. The session token discovery creates a hard data-layer dependency between auth and payments: auth extraction requires migrating session tokens out of the payments DB, which means touching the payments data layer before the payments service exists. Three sequencing options are now live: (A) Auth-first with session token migration as a sub-task — stand up auth service, migrate tokens out of payments DB using dual-write, complete auth extraction before payments work begins. Risk: touching payments DB early without payments service ownership is archaeologically dangerous. (B) Payments-first — extract payments service, take ownership of payments DB, then migrate session tokens to new auth DB as part of payments extraction. Auth service extracts second into a clean target. Risk: reverses the previously justified auth-first logic; payments is more complex and slower to extract. (C) Coordinated dual extraction — auth service stands up and proxies, but session token migration is explicitly deferred until payments DB is under payment service ownership; gap period runs with monolith reading tokens from payments DB under a named interim dependency. Option C is the current leading recommendation: it preserves auth-first's leverage advantage while making the inter-phase dependency explicit and time-bounded rather than pretending it doesn't exist. The specific week-by-week timeline from cycle 1 and the revised cycle 2 timeline are both now invalidated. Timeline reconstruction must wait for dependency mapping results and QSA input on session token scope. Strangler fig pattern still governs all extractions; deletion of old code paths remains part of done.

Coordinated dual extraction (Option C) is the current leading sequencing recommendation
Payments should be extracted early, not last — reinforced by cycle 3 finding that payments DB is load-bearing for auth
All extraction timelines are invalidated pending dependency mapping and QSA input
Fear-driven deferral of payments is an anti-pattern with compounding cost

strands.title: Coupling and Dependency Analysis
strands.content: Coupling profile has worsened each cycle through discovery, not revision of priors. Cycle 1: generic assumption of moderate coupling. Cycle 2: payments reaches into inventory (query), order state (write — hardest tentacle), and user profiles (read). Cycle 3: auth session tokens live in payments DB — a cross-domain data coupling that creates a circular extraction dependency. The pattern of accidental discovery in a 500K LOC codebase is itself a signal: the team does not have a reliable map of actual dependencies. Proceeding with extraction planning based on team intuition is now demonstrably risky. Static analysis tooling (jdeps, Structure101) is upgraded from recommended to required, and must complete before extraction work begins. Budget 2 weeks. Specific coupling tentacles now catalogued: (1) Payments→Inventory: stock validation query, likely synchronous, distributed transaction risk if blocking. (2) Payments→Order state: write dependency on transaction completion, hardest tentacle, requires event-driven boundary or internal API callback seam. (3) Payments→User profiles: billing data read, easiest, resolvable via read API. (4) Auth↔Payments DB: session tokens stored in payments DB, bidirectional operational dependency — auth needs the store, payments needs to own and isolate it. The dependency graph has a cycle at the data layer between auth and payments. This is the most urgent structural problem.

strands.title: Payment Service Design and PCI-DSS
strands.content: Third-party card vault (Stripe/Braintree/Adyen) for scope reduction remains the primary architectural driver — system should never handle raw PANs. Payment Service owns transaction records, payment status, refund logic; not card data. Network segmentation required from initial deployment. QSA engagement has been upgraded from week 8 to week 1-2: the session token discovery means the current architecture has a live PCI scope question (session tokens in CDE) that affects decisions being made now, not in month 2. The QSA needs to see the current state before extraction work begins. Framing for QSA: the migration is a compliance improvement — moving session tokens out of the CDE, reducing audit surface, isolating payment data properly. Session tokens in the payments DB may currently be pulling auth infrastructure into PCI scope; extraction fixes this. The payments DB cannot be handed to the payment service until session tokens are migrated out — this is a hard sequencing constraint and a critical path dependency. Payment Service API boundary remains narrow: initiatePayment(OrderId, PaymentToken, Amount), refund(TransactionId, Amount), getStatus(TransactionId). Internal OrderStateUpdate endpoint on monolith remains the interim pattern for the order state write dependency.

key_claims.text: Third-party card vault dramatically reduces PCI-DSS scope — system should never handle raw PANs
QSA must be engaged at week 1-2, not week 8 — session tokens in CDE is a live compliance question

Network segmentation for payment service is required from initial deployment

strands.title: Auth Service Design and Session Migration
strands.content: Auth service design from cycle 1 (Keycloak or Spring Authorization Server, JWT with 15-minute access tokens, longer refresh tokens, monolith delegates during transition) is preserved but blocked pending session token migration resolution. The session token migration is now the critical path item for auth extraction. Key constraints: (1) Uptime — session tokens were placed in the payments DB specifically for its uptime guarantees. The auth service DB must meet or exceed the same SLA; a default single-instance auth DB is not acceptable. This drives toward a managed high-availability DB (RDS Multi-AZ, Cloud Spanner, etc.) or Redis with replication for the session store. (2) Zero-downtime migration — session tokens are live stateful data. Naive cutover invalidates all active user sessions (mass logout). Required approach: dual-write to both old (payments DB) and new (auth DB) stores during transition, validate reads from new store, drain old store passively using token TTL, then cut reads over. Short token TTLs are a migration lever — if access tokens expire in 15 minutes, the old store drains naturally within one TTL window after dual-write is established. (3) Scope of migration — it is unknown whether session tokens are the only auth artifact in the payments DB. User credentials, API keys, or other auth data may also be present. This must be confirmed before migration scope is fixed.

key_claims.text: Auth service DB must match payments DB uptime SLA — default single-instance is not acceptable
Session token migration requires dual-write strategy to avoid mass user logout
Short JWT access token TTL (15 min) is a migration lever — natural drain from old store within one TTL window
Use Keycloak or Spring Authorization Server — do not build custom token management
Scope of auth artifacts in payments DB is unknown — may extend beyond session tokens

strands.title: Risk and Constraint Management
strands.content: Risk profile has escalated materially in cycle 3. Primary risk vectors: (1) Hidden coupling — the session token discovery was accidental. In a 500K LOC codebase, proceeding without tooling-based dependency mapping is now a named project risk, not just a best practice gap. (2) PCI scope creep — session tokens in the payments DB may currently be pulling auth infrastructure into the CDE. This is a live compliance problem, not a future one. (3) Timeline compression — coordinated dual extraction of auth and payments is more complex than independent sequential extraction. The 6-month timeline is under increased pressure. (4) Uptime regression — whatever replaces the payments DB as session store must preserve the uptime guarantee that justified the original design decision. Failing this introduces availability risk to auth. Technical debt inventory: shared DB for inventory/orders (named, intentional, phase 1), OrderStateUpdate internal API seam (named, intentional, retirement condition: orders extracted), interim gap period where monolith reads session tokens from payments DB during dual extraction (named, time-bounded). Team dynamics: the session token discovery validates the team's fear epistemically — payments really is dangerous. This should be acknowledged explicitly and used to build buy-in for the dependency mapping sprint. The discovery argues for the 2-week analysis hold, not for proceeding faster.

key_claims.text: Proceeding with extraction without tooling-based dependency mapping is now a named project risk
6-month timeline is under increased pressure due to coordinated dual extraction complexity
Session token discovery validates team fear epistemically — should be acknowledged and redirected, not dismissed
Without explicit feature routing rules, new features will regress into the monolith under deadline pressure
Parallel running of old and new code paths indefinitely is a primary migration failure mode

strands.title: System Profile
strands.content: 500K LOC Java monolith on Tomcat. Four domains: payments, inventory, user auth, order fulfillment. 6-month migration timeline, limited unquantified budget, PCI-DSS hard constraint. Coupling profile is materially worse than initial characterization: payments reaches into inventory, order state, and user profiles (cycle 2), payments DB is confirmed to store auth session tokens (cycle 3, empirical), and payments outage cascaded to auth and caused full application outage last quarter (cycle 4, empirical). That outage is the confirmed trigger for this migration. CEO has issued a hard 90-day constraint for auth independence from payments. This adds a second timeline: 90-day auth independence target running inside the 6-month full migration. Infrastructure details (cloud vs. on-prem, session token TTL) remain unconfirmed — these were explicitly solicited at end of cycle 4 and are critical path inputs for the dual-write migration plan.

strands.title: 90-Day Auth Independence Target
strands.content: New strand — CEO-mandated hard constraint from cycle 4. The 90-day target is achievable but only under specific scoping and resourcing conditions. Correct interpretation: auth service extracted, session tokens on dedicated HA store, payments outage cannot cascade to auth — this is testable and demoable. Wrong interpretation: full auth + payments separation with clean PCI boundaries — that is 6-month work. The CEO definition must be locked in writing within the first two weeks or the target will be evaluated against the wrong scope at day 89. Proposed 90-day sequence: Weeks 1-2 dependency mapping + QSA emergency engagement + auth DB provisioning; Weeks 3-6 session token dual-write migration; Weeks 7-10 auth service extraction proper + Resilience4j circuit breakers; Weeks 11-12 cutover, chaos testing, validation. Critical failure modes for the 90-day target: (1) dependency mapping skipped to save time — this is how mid-extraction surprises blow the timeline; (2) team splits focus between auth extraction and other work — requires dedicated 2-3 engineer squad protected from context-switching; (3) auth DB uptime SLA not resolved in week 1 — the uptime rationale for payments DB must be matched or the problem is just relocated; (4) QSA engagement becomes a blocker — must be framed as scoping confirmation (2-week turnaround), not a full assessment. Circuit breaker work (Resilience4j) is part of the 90-day delivery, not an afterthought — the cascade failure was a bulkheading problem, not only a co-location problem.
depends_on: Auth Service Design and Session Migration

The coordinated dual extraction (Option C) remains structurally correct but the gap period is now time-bounded by the 90-day target, not open-ended. Payments extraction begins after auth independence is confirmed — this is now the explicit sequencing: auth independence by day 90, then payments extraction in the remaining ~3 months of the 6-month window. Inventory and order fulfillment remain deferred to phase 2 as previously established. The internal OrderStateUpdate API seam for payments→order state write dependency remains the interim pattern. All previously invalidated week-by-week timelines remain invalidated; the 90-day sequence in the new strand replaces them for the auth phase. Payments phase timeline requires reconstruction after auth work is underway and dependency mapping results are available. Strangler fig pattern still governs all extractions.

Cycle 2: payments reaches into inventory (query), order state (write — hardest tentacle), and user profiles (read). Cycle 3: auth session tokens live in payments DB — cyclic data-layer dependency. Cycle 4: payments outage cascade to auth confirmed empirically — the coupling is not just architectural, it has a proven operational blast radius. (3) Payments→User profiles: billing data read, easiest, resolvable via read API. (4) Auth↔Payments DB: session tokens in payments DB, bidirectional operational dependency, confirmed blast radius.

strands.content: Third-party card vault (Stripe/Braintree/Adyen) for scope reduction remains the primary architectural driver. strands.title: Auth Service Design and Session Migration
strands.content: Auth service extraction is now the primary 90-day deliverable. Design parameters from prior cycles preserved: Keycloak or Spring Authorization Server, JWT with 15-minute access tokens, longer refresh tokens, monolith delegates during transition. Session token migration is the critical path item and the first execution risk. Key constraints: (1) Uptime — auth service DB must meet or exceed the payments DB SLA. This is not optional; the original placement of tokens in payments DB was explicitly uptime-motivated. Managed HA DB required: RDS Multi-AZ, Cloud Spanner, or Redis Cluster with replication. The specific infrastructure choice depends on whether the team is on cloud or on-prem — this is an open question explicitly solicited in cycle 4. (2) Zero-downtime migration via dual-write: write tokens to both payments DB and new auth DB simultaneously, validate reads from new store in shadow mode, drain old store passively using token TTL, cut reads over only after validation. Short token TTLs are a migration lever — 15-minute access tokens drain naturally within one TTL window after dual-write is established. (3) Scope of auth artifacts in payments DB is still unconfirmed — session tokens may not be the only auth data present. Must confirm before migration scope is fixed. (4) Circuit breakers (Resilience4j) must be in place before or during cutover — the outage was a cascade failure, not just a co-location problem. Bulkheads are part of the auth independence deliverable.

Primary risk vectors: (1) Hidden coupling — session token discovery was accidental; cascade failure confirms coupling has real operational blast radius. Dependency mapping is a hard prerequisite. (2) PCI scope creep — session tokens in CDE is live. (3) Timeline compression — 90-day target is tight; two timelines now running (90-day auth independence + 6-month full migration). (4) Uptime regression — auth DB must match payments DB SLA or the problem is relocated, not solved. (5) CEO expectation mismatch — if CEO's mental model is '90 days = full migration done', that must be corrected now. The cascade failure also reframes the team's fear: it was epistemically correct. Payments is dangerous and proven so. Use this to build buy-in for dependency mapping sprint — 'we found the session token problem by accident; the outage proves there's more we don't know.' Technical debt inventory: shared DB for inventory/orders (named, intentional, phase 1), OrderStateUpdate internal API seam (named, intentional, retirement condition: orders extracted), gap period where monolith reads session tokens from payments DB during dual-write phase (named, time-bounded to weeks 3-6). Feature routing rule remains critical: new features touching payments or auth must go into new services, not back into the monolith.

strands.content: Third-party card vault (Stripe/Braintree/Adyen) for scope reduction remains the primary architectural driver. QSA engagement is now a week 1-2 requirement — framed as emergency scoping confirmation, not a full assessment, with an explicit 2-week turnaround expectation. The session tokens in CDE is a live compliance question and a potential audit surface problem that predates the migration. Migration framing for QSA: this is a compliance improvement — moving session tokens out of the CDE, reducing audit surface, isolating payment data. The payments DB cannot be handed to the payment service until session tokens are migrated out — hard sequencing constraint. Network segmentation required from initial deployment. Payment Service API boundary: initiatePayment(OrderId, PaymentToken, Amount), refund(TransactionId, Amount), getStatus(TransactionId). Internal OrderStateUpdate endpoint on monolith remains the interim pattern for order state write dependency. Payments extraction is explicitly post-90-day-auth-target work — this gives the team ~3 months after auth independence to execute payments extraction, which is tight given coupling complexity.

key_claims.text: Third-party card vault dramatically reduces PCI-DSS scope — system should never handle raw PANs
QSA must be engaged at week 1-2 as emergency scoping confirmation with 2-week turnaround expectation
Payments DB cannot be handed to payment service until session tokens are migrated out — hard sequencing constraint
Network segmentation for payment service is required from initial deployment

strands.title: Auth Service Design and Session Migration
strands.content: Auth service extraction is now the primary 90-day deliverable. Design parameters from prior cycles preserved: Keycloak or Spring Authorization Server, JWT with 15-minute access tokens, longer refresh tokens, monolith delegates during transition. Session token migration is the critical path item and the first execution risk. Key constraints: (1) Uptime — auth service DB must meet or exceed the payments DB SLA. This is not optional; the original placement of tokens in payments DB was explicitly uptime-motivated. Managed HA DB required: RDS Multi-AZ, Cloud Spanner, or Redis Cluster with replication. The specific infrastructure choice depends on whether the team is on cloud or on-prem — this is an open question explicitly solicited in cycle 4. (2) Zero-downtime migration via dual-write: write tokens to both payments DB and new auth DB simultaneously, validate reads from new store in shadow mode, drain old store passively using token TTL, cut reads over only after validation. Short token TTLs are a migration lever — 15-minute access tokens drain naturally within one TTL window after dual-write is established. (3) Scope of auth artifacts in payments DB is still unconfirmed — session tokens may not be the only auth data present. Must confirm before migration scope is fixed. (4) Circuit breakers (Resilience4j) must be in place before or during cutover — the outage was a cascade failure, not just a co-location problem. Bulkheads are part of the auth independence deliverable.

key_claims.text: Auth service DB must match payments DB uptime SLA — default single-instance is not acceptable
Session token migration requires dual-write strategy to avoid mass user logout
Short JWT access token TTL (15 min) is a migration lever — natural drain from old store within one TTL window
Use Keycloak or Spring Authorization Server — do not build custom token management
Scope of auth artifacts in payments DB is unknown — may extend beyond session tokens
Circuit breakers are part of the auth independence deliverable, not post-migration polish

strands.title: Risk and Constraint Management
strands.content: Risk profile has a new confirmed data point: the cascade failure is empirical, not theoretical. This changes the risk register from speculative to evidenced. Primary risk vectors: (1) Hidden coupling — session token discovery was accidental; cascade failure confirms coupling has real operational blast radius. Dependency mapping is a hard prerequisite. (2) PCI scope creep — session tokens in CDE is live. (3) Timeline compression — 90-day target is tight; two timelines now running (90-day auth independence + 6-month full migration). (4) Uptime regression — auth DB must match payments DB SLA or the problem is relocated, not solved. (5) CEO expectation mismatch — if CEO's mental model is '90 days = full migration done', that must be corrected now. The cascade failure also reframes the team's fear: it was epistemically correct. Payments is dangerous and proven so. Use this to build buy-in for dependency mapping sprint — 'we found the session token problem by accident; the outage proves there's more we don't know.' Technical debt inventory: shared DB for inventory/orders (named, intentional, phase 1), OrderStateUpdate internal API seam (named, intentional, retirement condition: orders extracted), gap period where monolith reads session tokens from payments DB during dual-write phase (named, time-bounded to weeks 3-6). Feature routing rule remains critical: new features touching payments or auth must go into new services, not back into the monolith.

key_claims.text: Cascade failure is empirically confirmed — risk profile upgrades from speculative to evidenced
Proceeding with extraction without tooling-based dependency mapping is a named project risk
CEO expectation mismatch on 90-day scope is a top-3 project risk — must be resolved in week 1
6-month timeline is under increased pressure — two nested timelines now running
Without explicit feature routing rules, new features will regress into the monolith under deadline pressure
Parallel running of old and new code paths indefinitely is a primary migration failure mode
