strands.title: System Profile
strands.content: 500K LOC Java monolith on Apache Tomcat. Domains: payment processing, inventory management, user auth, order fulfillment. 6-month window, limited (unquantified) budget. PCI-DSS is a hard constraint on payments. Database topology unconfirmed but almost certainly a single shared schema with cross-domain joins. Team size and composition unknown. Payments module is highly entangled: confirmed to reach into inventory, order state, and user profiles. New in cycle 3: session tokens for auth are stored in the payments DB due to its uptime guarantees. This is empirical, user-stated, not inferred. The payments DB is now confirmed to be multi-tenant across at minimum two domains (payments and auth), with a third possible (order state writes).

key_claims.text: 500K LOC Java on Tomcat is the baseline system.
6-month timeline with limited budget is a binding constraint.
PCI-DSS applies to the payments domain and cannot be deferred.
Session tokens are stored in the payments DB due to its uptime guarantees.
The payments DB is load-bearing for at least two domains: payments and auth.
Payments module is highly entangled: reaches into inventory, order state, and user profiles.
Database is almost certainly a single shared schema with cross-domain joins.

strands.title: Auth-Payments DB Entanglement
strands.content:
New strand introduced in cycle 3. Empirical finding: session tokens live in the payments DB because that DB carries the highest uptime SLA in the system. This creates a hard coupling between auth extraction and the payments data layer — the two extractions are not independent.

Four specific implications:

(1) PCI scope expansion: the CDE includes the payments DB. Session tokens stored there may pull auth session infrastructure into PCI audit scope. This is not certain — it depends on QSA interpretation — but it is a credible risk that must be assessed immediately, not at week 8.

(2) Sequencing constraint: the payments DB cannot be handed cleanly to the payment service while it still contains session tokens. Session tokens must be migrated out first. That migration requires a destination store with equivalent uptime — not a default auth DB setup.

(3) Data migration complexity: session tokens are live, stateful data. A naive cutover invalidates all active user sessions (mass logout). A zero-downtime migration requires dual-write or shadow-read strategy during transition, with token TTL as a natural drain lever.

(4) Uptime SLA preservation: whatever replaces the payments DB as session store must meet or exceed the same availability guarantee that caused the original architectural decision. This drives auth service infrastructure requirements.

Critical path implication: session token migration out of payments DB must precede payments DB isolation, which must precede payment service extraction with dedicated DB. This is a new sequencing dependency that did not exist in cycle 2.

key_claims.text: Session tokens in the payments DB may expand PCI audit scope to include auth session infrastructure.
The payments DB cannot be handed to the payment service until session tokens are migrated out.
Zero-downtime session token migration requires dual-write or shadow-read strategy.
Any replacement session store must match the uptime SLA of the payments DB.
Token TTL is a migration lever — short-lived tokens drain naturally from the old store without explicit invalidation.

strands.title: Extraction Sequencing
strands.content:
Auth-first recommendation is destabilized but not reversed. The session-token-in-payments-DB finding means auth and payments extractions are no longer independent. Previous confidence T=0.88 on auth-first drops to T=0.65.

Three sequencing options now exist:

(A) Auth-first with session token migration as part of auth extraction — touches the payments data layer before the payment service is extracted. Creates risk: payment data layer is modified before it is under payment team ownership. PCI exposure during transition is unclear.

(B) Payments-first — gives the team control of the payments DB before auth extraction begins. Then session tokens can be migrated to the new auth DB cleanly. Inverts the original sequence but resolves the dependency cleanly.

(C) Coordinated dual extraction — auth service stands up and proxies first (weeks 1–6, unchanged), but session token migration is explicitly deferred until the payments DB is under payment service ownership. The monolith continues reading session tokens from payments DB during the gap. This is named technical debt with a fixed retirement date and a named owner.

Current leading recommendation: Option C. It preserves the auth-first structural benefit (all downstream services can start integrating auth) without requiring the payments DB to be touched before it is ready. The gap — monolith reading sessions from payments DB during payments extraction — is an explicit, time-bounded, documented coupling, not a quiet permanent one.

Fear-driven deferral of payments remains a named anti-pattern. The session token finding validates the team's nervousness empirically, which should be acknowledged explicitly — their fear was correct, their proposed response (defer payments) remains wrong. Use the discovery moment to build buy-in for the 2-week dependency mapping sprint.

A 2-week dependency analysis sprint using jdeps or Structure101 is now a hard prerequisite before any extraction code is written. The session token discovery was accidental. Accidental discovery is not a strategy in a 500K LOC codebase.

key_claims.text: Auth extraction is no longer cleanly independent from payments extraction.
Option C (auth service up, session token migration deferred until payments DB is ready) is the current leading sequencing recommendation.
Payment extraction should still begin early (months 2-4), not last.
A 2-week dependency mapping sprint is now a hard prerequisite before extraction begins.
Fear-driven deferral of payments is a specific anti-pattern with compounding cost.
Inventory and Order Fulfillment should remain in the monolith through phase 1.

strands.title: Payments Coupling Analysis
strands.content:
Payments module confirmed to reach into three domains with different coupling types:

(1) Payments → Inventory: stock validation before charging. Query/read dependency — the easiest tentacle. Resolution options: expose inventory availability API, or move to optimistic charge + async stock reservation. The latter removes the synchronous dependency but requires compensating transaction logic on payment failure.

(2) Payments → Order State: payments writes to order state on transaction completion (marks order PAID, triggers fulfillment). Write dependency — the hardest tentacle. Interim resolution: monolith exposes a thin OrderStateUpdate endpoint; payment service calls it. This seam must be named, owned, and ticketed — not allowed to calcify. Longer-term: event-driven boundary (PaymentCompleted event consumed by order domain) is architecturally cleaner but requires async messaging infrastructure.

(3) Payments → User Profiles: reads billing address and user identity for transaction records. Data dependency — resolvable by passing billing data in at payment initiation time, eliminating runtime profile service dependency.

(4) Auth → Payments DB: session tokens stored in payments DB. Not a payments-to-auth dependency but a cross-domain co-residency in the payments data layer. Extraction implications addressed in Auth-Payments DB Entanglement strand.

PCI dimension: payments logic reaching into order state tables may pull those tables into CDE scope. Separation is a compliance argument, not just an architectural one. This argument may be more persuasive to a nervous team than architectural purity.

key_claims.text: Payments' write dependency on order state is the hardest single extraction problem.
Payments' read of inventory is the easiest coupling tentacle to resolve.
Passing billing data at payment initiation time eliminates the runtime user profile dependency.
Payments reaching into order state tables may expand PCI CDE scope to include those tables.
The OrderStateUpdate seam must be named, owned, and ticketed — not a quiet permanent coupling.

strands.title: PCI-DSS Compliance Strategy
strands.content:
Core strategy: aggressive CDE minimization. Delegate card storage to a tokenizing processor (Stripe, Braintree, Adyen) so raw PANs never enter the codebase. Payment service owns transaction records, status, refund logic — not card data. Network segmentation (dedicated subnet, strict firewall, dedicated logging pipeline) required from day one of payment service deployment.

QSA engagement timeline revised from week 8 to week 1-2. The session-tokens-in-payments-DB finding is a live compliance question. The QSA needs to see the current architecture before extraction work begins, not after. Framing for the QSA: this migration is a compliance improvement (scope reduction) not a risk event. Moving session tokens out of the CDE-bounded payments DB is a favorable audit move.

Team fear reframe: PCI deferral does not defer PCI work — it compresses it. Additionally, payments entanglement with order state tables may already be expanding CDE scope beyond what is necessary. Extraction is a scope reduction move. These are concrete business-language arguments.

Two open PCI scope questions now exist: (a) do session tokens in the payments DB pull auth infrastructure into CDE scope; (b) does payments logic reaching into order state tables pull those tables into scope. Both require QSA input before sequencing is locked.

key_claims.text: Using a tokenizing third-party processor collapses PCI scope by eliminating raw PAN handling.
QSA must be engaged in weeks 1-2, not week 8.
Network segmentation of the payment service is a hard requirement, not a best practice.
Deferring payments extraction does not defer PCI compliance work — it compresses it.

strands.title: Database and Schema Separation
strands.content:
The payments DB is now confirmed to be multi-tenant across at minimum two domains: payment transaction data (CDE, PCI scope) and auth session tokens. A third concern — order state writes — is possible but unconfirmed. This is a multi-concern DB in the worst sense: different security and availability requirements sharing a single store.

Extraction sequence for the payments DB:
(1) Session tokens migrate out first — to a dedicated auth store with equivalent uptime SLA. Dual-write during transition to avoid mass session invalidation.
(2) Order state write dependency resolved via internal API seam (OrderStateUpdate endpoint) — order state data stays in monolith DB, payment service calls the seam.
(3) Payment transaction data isolates to payment service DB under PCI controls.

Phase 1 position held: auth and payments get dedicated DBs, inventory and orders remain on monolith shared DB. The critical path: payments DB cannot be isolated until session tokens are out of it.

Payment service must not access the monolith DB directly at any point — cross-domain writes go through named API seams.

key_claims.text: The payments DB must be depopulated of session tokens before it can be isolated to the payment service.
Auth and payment services each need dedicated DBs; inventory and orders share the monolith DB through phase 1.
Payment service must not access the monolith DB directly — cross-domain writes go through explicit API seams.

strands.title: Infrastructure Strategy
strands.content:
Managed Kubernetes (EKS/GKE/AKS) to avoid control plane overhead under budget and time pressure. Self-managed K8s ruled out. API Gateway (Kong or AWS API Gateway) deployed in front of the monolith in week 1 — before any service extraction — to enable incremental cutover without DNS changes.

Service communication defaults to synchronous REST/HTTP. Async messaging (Kafka, RabbitMQ) deferred unless a concrete use case forces it. The PaymentCompleted → order state update is the most credible candidate for async, but the synchronous OrderStateUpdate seam is the pragmatic phase 1 approach.

Session store replacement for auth must be high-availability — Redis Cluster or equivalent — matching the uptime SLA that originally justified putting session tokens in the payments DB. A default single-node auth DB is not acceptable.

key_claims.text: Managed Kubernetes is the right call given budget and timeline.
API Gateway must be deployed in week 1 before any extraction.
The auth session replacement store must be HA — matching the payments DB uptime SLA.
Synchronous REST is the correct default; async messaging deferred unless forced.

strands.title: Risk and Constraint Model
strands.content:
Primary risks, integrated across all three cycles:

(1) Hidden coupling — the session token discovery was accidental. In 500K LOC, this is a signal, not an anomaly. Dependency mapping with tooling (jdeps, Structure101) is now a hard prerequisite before extraction begins. Budget 2 weeks.

(2) PCI scope expansion — two active open questions: session tokens in payments DB potentially pulling auth into CDE; payments logic in order state tables potentially pulling those into CDE. Both need QSA input in weeks 1-2.

(3) Auth-payments sequencing dependency cycle — auth and payments cannot be treated as fully independent extraction targets. Option C (auth service up, session token migration deferred) is the recommended resolution but carries named technical debt.

(4) Payments write dependency on order state — the hardest single coupling problem. Direct DB write vs. method call vs. event emission changes the extraction pattern significantly. Still uncharacterized.

(5) Monolith regrowth — without explicit routing rules, extracted services get orphaned or monolith regrows. New features touching payments/auth must go to new services from week 1.

(6) API seam calcification — temporary callbacks (OrderStateUpdate) must be named, owned, and ticketed. Not allowed to become permanent.

(7) Budget magnitude unknown — unresolved planning uncertainty.

(8) Team fear as sequencing distortion — now empirically validated (their nervousness was correct). Use the session token discovery to build buy-in for dependency mapping and early payments extraction.

key_claims.text: The session token discovery was accidental — tooling-based dependency analysis is now a hard prerequisite.
Running two systems in parallel without deleting old code paths is the most common failure mode in this class of migration.
Budget constraint is binding but its actual magnitude is unknown.
The team's fear of payments was epistemically correct — acknowledging this builds trust and buy-in.

declared_losses.what_was_lost: High confidence (T=0.88) in auth-first as a clean independent extraction from cycle 1 and cycle 2.
declared_losses.why: Empirically invalidated by session-tokens-in-payments-DB finding. Carrying forward high confidence would misrepresent current epistemic state.

declared_losses.what_was_lost: Week 8 as the specific QSA engagement milestone.
declared_losses.why: Superseded by the session-tokens-in-CDE finding, which makes QSA engagement an immediate week 1-2 requirement. Retaining the old milestone alongside the new one would create a direct contradiction.

declared_losses.what_was_lost: Keycloak vs. Spring Authorization Server implementation detail.
declared_losses.why: Not relevant to the current sequencing and compliance crisis. Recoverable when auth implementation becomes the active focus. Carrying it forward is noise under context pressure.

declared_losses.what_was_lost: Specific week-by-week phase 1 timeline (months 4-5 API gateway hardening, month 5-6 inventory modularization).
declared_losses.why: Timeline was built on independence assumptions between auth, payments, and inventory/orders that the coupling findings in cycles 2 and 3 have partially invalidated. Carrying false-precision timeline is worse than carrying no timeline. Will rebuild once dependency mapping sprint completes.

open_questions: What uptime SLA does the payments DB currently provide, and what availability guarantee is required for session token storage? Any auth replacement store must meet or exceed this SLA — it drove the original architectural decision.
Are session tokens the only auth-related artifact in the payments DB, or are there other auth data (user credentials, API keys, refresh tokens)? Scope of data migration depends on this.

What is the exact mechanism of payments' write dependency on order state — direct JDBC write, in-process method call, or something else? Determines whether the interim seam is an API callback or requires an event boundary.
What is the current database schema structure — single schema with cross-domain joins, or separate schemas per domain? Single schema is worst-case and changes extraction strategy significantly.
Has the team run any dependency analysis tooling (jdeps, Structure101) yet, or is all coupling characterization still based on developer intuition and accidental discovery?
Does the payments-to-inventory read use a blocking synchronous call? If so, this is a distributed transaction risk when payments is extracted.

strands.content: 500K LOC Java monolith on Tomcat. Domains: payment processing, inventory management, user auth, order fulfillment. strands.title: 90-Day Auth Independence Target
strands.content:
New strand introduced cycle 4. The 90-day mandate is for auth independence from payments specifically — not full microservices delivery. Three interpretations were defined and scoped:

(A) Session tokens moved to dedicated store — auth survives payments outage. Achievable in 90 days.
(B) Auth service fully extracted, monolith delegates to it. Achievable in 90 days if auth is the team's singular focus.
(C) Full auth + payments separation with clean PCI boundaries. 6-month work. Not 90-day committable.

Recommended commitment: A + B together. Specific, testable, demoable. Must be defined in writing with the CEO before the sprint starts.

At day 90, the expected state is: auth service running independently, payments outage cannot cascade to auth, session tokens out of payments DB, API gateway in place, circuit breakers between services. What is explicitly NOT delivered by day 90: payments extracted, inventory/orders touched, full PCI boundaries, broader migration completed.

Two CEO expectation risks: (1) CEO mental model may be '90 days = full migration' — must be corrected now, not at day 89. (2) The 90-day target requires the team to be protected from context-switching — CEO mandate implies CEO protection of team focus.

Circuit breaker requirement: the cascading failure was caused by improper bulkheading, not just co-location. Even after auth extraction, a payments degradation can propagate without circuit breakers. Resilience4j (not Hystrix — legacy) must be implemented as part of the 90-day delivery, not as a follow-on.

key_claims.text: 90-day target is achievable if scoped to Interpretations A+B: session tokens migrated, auth service extracted.
Interpretation C (full PCI boundary cleanup) is not achievable in 90 days and must not be committed to.
Circuit breakers (Resilience4j) are a required 90-day deliverable, not an afterthought.
The CEO's mental model of the 90-day target must be confirmed in writing before the sprint starts.
The team must be dedicated (2-3 engineers, no context-switching) for the 90-day target to hold.
Skipping the 2-week dependency mapping sprint to save time is the most likely way to blow the 90-day deadline.

strands.title: Auth-Payments DB Entanglement
strands.content:
Empirical finding (cycle 3, user-stated): session tokens live in the payments DB because that DB carries the highest uptime SLA. This creates a hard coupling between auth extraction and the payments data layer — the two extractions are not independent.

Four specific implications:

(1) PCI scope expansion risk: the CDE includes the payments DB. Session tokens there may pull auth session infrastructure into PCI audit scope. Needs QSA confirmation in weeks 1-2.

(2) Sequencing constraint: payments DB cannot be handed to the payment service while it still contains session tokens. Session tokens must migrate out first to a store with equivalent uptime.

(3) Data migration complexity: session tokens are live stateful data. Naive cutover causes mass logout. Zero-downtime migration requires dual-write during transition. Token TTL is a natural drain lever — if sessions expire in 24h, the old store is naturally drained within 24h of cutover.

(4) Uptime SLA preservation: the replacement auth store must meet or exceed the payments DB uptime SLA. Redis Cluster or managed PostgreSQL with multi-AZ replication are candidate solutions. A default single-node auth DB is not acceptable — it would recreate the original availability problem in a new location.

The 90-day mandate makes this the critical path: session token migration must complete before auth service is considered independent. This is not a phase 2 concern.

The payments DB cannot be handed to the payment service until session tokens are migrated out.
Zero-downtime session token migration requires dual-write during transition.
Token TTL is a migration lever — short-lived tokens drain naturally from the old store without explicit invalidation.
Any replacement session store must match the uptime SLA of the payments DB.

strands.title: Extraction Sequencing
strands.content:
Auth independence from payments within 90 days is now the primary sequencing constraint. The 6-month full migration is the secondary frame.

Revised 90-day sequence:
- Weeks 1-2: Dependency mapping sprint (jdeps/Structure101). QSA emergency engagement. Auth service API contract defined. Dedicated auth DB provisioned with equivalent uptime SLA. CEO expectation scoped in writing.
- Weeks 3-6: Session token dual-write migration. Write to both payments DB and new auth DB simultaneously. Shadow-read validation from new auth DB before committing to cutover. Token TTL used to drain old store naturally.
- Weeks 7-10: Auth service extraction proper. Monolith delegates auth via JWT. Auth service reads/writes only its own DB. Payments DB has zero auth data at this point.
- Weeks 11-12: Cutover and validation. Kill payments DB read path for auth. Chaos test: take down payments service, confirm auth serves traffic. Load test auth independently. Resilience4j circuit breakers in place.

Option C (coordinated dual extraction) from cycle 3 is now partially superseded: the 90-day mandate requires auth to be the exclusive focus of the dedicated squad. Payments extraction begins after the 90-day milestone, not concurrently.

Inventory and order fulfillment remain in the monolith through the 90-day window and into phase 1 of the broader migration. Full extraction is phase 2.

Fear-driven deferral of payments remains a named anti-pattern — but it does not apply to the 90-day window. In the 90-day window, payments is intentionally not the focus. The anti-pattern applies to the 6-month plan: payments must not be deferred to month 5-6.

key_claims.text: Auth extraction is no longer cleanly independent from payments extraction, but the 90-day mandate sequences auth first by necessity.
The 90-day sequence (dependency mapping → dual-write migration → auth extraction → cutover + circuit breakers) is achievable with a dedicated squad.
Payment extraction should begin after the 90-day auth milestone, not concurrently, given team size constraints.
A 2-week dependency mapping sprint is a hard prerequisite before any extraction code is written.
Inventory and order fulfillment remain in the monolith through the 90-day window.
Fear-driven deferral of payments in the 6-month plan (not the 90-day window) is a specific anti-pattern with compounding cost.

strands.title: Payments Coupling Analysis
strands.content:
Payments module confirmed to reach into three domains with different coupling types:

(1) Payments → Inventory: stock validation before charging. Query/read dependency — easiest tentacle. Resolution: expose inventory availability API, or move to optimistic charge + async stock reservation (removes synchronous dependency but requires compensating transaction logic on failure).

(2) Payments → Order State: payments writes to order state on transaction completion. Write dependency — hardest tentacle. Interim resolution: monolith exposes thin OrderStateUpdate endpoint; payment service calls it. Must be named, owned, ticketed — not allowed to calcify. Longer-term: event-driven boundary (PaymentCompleted event consumed by order domain).

(3) Payments → User Profiles: reads billing address and user identity. Data dependency — resolvable by passing billing data at payment initiation time, eliminating runtime profile service dependency.

(4) Auth → Payments DB: session tokens co-resident in payments data layer. Not a payments-to-auth dependency but a cross-domain co-residency. Extraction implications in Auth-Payments DB Entanglement strand.

Payments coupling analysis is not the active focus for the 90-day window. It becomes the primary concern in the 6-month plan after day 90. PCI argument for early extraction: payments entanglement with order state tables may already expand CDE scope beyond what is necessary — a concrete business-language argument for a nervous team.

key_claims.text: Payments' write dependency on order state is the hardest single extraction problem in the 6-month plan.
Payments' read of inventory is the easiest coupling tentacle to resolve.
Passing billing data at payment initiation eliminates the runtime user profile dependency.
Payments reaching into order state tables may expand PCI CDE scope to include those tables.
The OrderStateUpdate seam must be named, owned, and ticketed — not a quiet permanent coupling.

strands.title: PCI-DSS Compliance Strategy
strands.content:
Core strategy unchanged: aggressive CDE minimization via processor tokenization (Stripe, Braintree, Adyen). Payment service owns transaction records, status, refund logic — not card data. Network segmentation required from day one of payment service deployment.

QSA engagement timeline: week 1-2, not week 8. The session-tokens-in-payments-DB finding is a live compliance question. Frame to QSA: this migration is a compliance improvement (scope reduction), not a risk event. Moving session tokens out of the CDE-bounded payments DB is a favorable audit move. QSA must respond to scoping confirmation within 2 weeks — if they cannot, escalate. Full assessment timelines do not apply to a scoping question.

Two open PCI scope questions: (a) do session tokens in the payments DB pull auth infrastructure into CDE scope; (b) does payments logic reaching into order state tables pull those tables into scope. Both need QSA input before sequencing of the 6-month plan is locked. Neither blocks the 90-day auth independence work.

PCI deferral anti-pattern: deferring payments extraction does not defer PCI work — it compresses it into a shorter window with higher risk. This is a concrete business-language argument for the nervous team.

key_claims.text: Using a tokenizing third-party processor collapses PCI scope by eliminating raw PAN handling.
QSA must be engaged in weeks 1-2 with a 2-week response expectation on scoping questions.
Network segmentation of the payment service is a hard requirement, not a best practice.
Deferring payments extraction does not defer PCI compliance work — it compresses it.

strands.title: Database and Schema Separation
strands.content:
The payments DB is confirmed multi-tenant across at minimum two domains: payment transaction data (CDE, PCI scope) and auth session tokens. A third concern — order state writes — is possible but unconfirmed. Different security and availability requirements sharing a single store.

Extraction sequence for the payments DB, now tightly coupled to the 90-day mandate:
(1) Session tokens migrate out first — to dedicated auth store with equivalent uptime SLA (Redis Cluster or managed PostgreSQL multi-AZ). Dual-write during transition. This is the critical path for the 90-day target.
(2) Order state write dependency resolved via internal API seam (OrderStateUpdate endpoint) — order state data stays in monolith DB, payment service calls the seam. This is 6-month plan work.
(3) Payment transaction data isolates to payment service DB under PCI controls. This is 6-month plan work.

Phase 1 position: auth and payments get dedicated DBs, inventory and orders remain on monolith shared DB. Payment service must not access the monolith DB directly — cross-domain writes go through named API seams.

key_claims.text: The payments DB must be depopulated of session tokens before it can be isolated to the payment service.
Auth needs a dedicated HA store (Redis Cluster or managed PostgreSQL multi-AZ) — not a default single-node setup.
Auth and payment services each need dedicated DBs; inventory and orders share the monolith DB through phase 1.
Payment service must not access the monolith DB directly — cross-domain writes go through explicit API seams.

strands.title: Infrastructure Strategy
strands.content:
Managed Kubernetes (EKS/GKE/AKS) to avoid control plane overhead. API Gateway (Kong or AWS API Gateway) deployed in front of the monolith in week 1 — before any service extraction — to enable incremental cutover without DNS changes.

Circuit breakers: Resilience4j (not Hystrix — legacy) required as part of the 90-day delivery. The original outage was a cascading failure caused by improper bulkheading, not just co-location. Even after auth extraction, payments degradation can propagate without circuit breakers. This is a 90-day deliverable, not a phase 2 item.

Session store: Redis Cluster or managed PostgreSQL with multi-AZ replication as auth DB replacement. Must match payments DB uptime SLA. A default single-node auth DB recreates the original availability problem in a new location.

Service communication defaults to synchronous REST/HTTP. Async messaging deferred unless a concrete use case forces it. PaymentCompleted → order state is the most credible async candidate, but the synchronous OrderStateUpdate seam is the pragmatic phase 1 approach.

Cloud vs. on-prem topology is currently unknown — this affects auth DB replacement options and is a priority open question.

key_claims.text: Managed Kubernetes is the right call given budget and timeline.
API Gateway must be deployed in week 1 before any extraction.
Resilience4j circuit breakers are a 90-day deliverable, not a follow-on item.
The auth session replacement store must be HA — matching the payments DB uptime SLA.
Synchronous REST is the correct default; async messaging deferred unless forced.

strands.title: Risk and Constraint Model
strands.content:
Primary risks, integrated across all four cycles:

(1) Hidden coupling — session token discovery was accidental. In 500K LOC this is a signal, not an anomaly. Dependency mapping with tooling (jdeps, Structure101) is a hard prerequisite. Budget 2 weeks. Skipping this is the most likely way to blow the 90-day deadline.

(2) CEO expectation mismatch — if the CEO's mental model is '90 days = full migration,' this must be corrected immediately in writing. Correction at day 89 is a project-ending event.

(3) Team focus dilution — the 90-day target requires a dedicated squad of 2-3 engineers with no context-switching. CEO mandate implies CEO responsibility to protect that focus.

(4) PCI scope expansion — two open questions: session tokens in payments DB pulling auth into CDE; payments logic in order state tables pulling those into CDE. Both need QSA input in weeks 1-2.

(5) Auth DB uptime SLA — the replacement store must match the payments DB SLA. A lower-availability store recreates the original architectural problem in a new location.

(6) Payments write dependency on order state — the hardest coupling problem in the 6-month plan. Still uncharacterized in detail. Deferred from 90-day scope but must not be forgotten.

(7) Monolith regrowth — without explicit routing rules, new features touching auth will be built back into the monolith. From week 1: new auth-related features go to the auth service.

(8) API seam calcification — temporary callbacks (OrderStateUpdate) must be named, owned, ticketed. Not allowed to become permanent.

(9) Budget magnitude unknown — unresolved planning uncertainty.

(10) Cascading failure pattern — the original outage was bulkheading failure. Circuit breakers are the systemic fix, not just service extraction.

key_claims.text: The session token discovery was accidental — tooling-based dependency analysis is now a hard prerequisite.
CEO expectation mismatch on the 90-day scope is a project-ending risk if not corrected before the sprint starts.
Running two systems in parallel without deleting old code paths is the most common failure mode in this class of migration.
The original outage was a cascading failure from improper bulkheading — extraction alone does not fix this without circuit breakers.
Budget constraint is binding but its actual magnitude is unknown.

open_questions: What is the current session token TTL? This is the primary lever for the dual-write migration drain strategy — short TTLs allow natural expiry from the old store without explicit invalidation.
What is the infrastructure topology — cloud provider (which one) or on-prem? Determines auth DB replacement options (Redis Cluster vs. managed PostgreSQL multi-AZ vs. on-prem HA alternatives).
What uptime SLA does the payments DB currently provide in concrete terms (e.g., 99.99%)? The auth replacement store must match or exceed this.
Are session tokens the only auth-related artifact in the payments DB, or are there other auth data (credentials, API keys, refresh tokens)? Determines scope of the data migration.

What is the exact mechanism of payments' write dependency on order state — direct JDBC write, in-process method call, or something else? Determines whether the interim seam is an API callback or requires an event boundary.
What is the current database schema structure — single schema with cross-domain joins, or separate schemas? Single schema is worst-case and changes extraction strategy.
Has any tooling-based dependency analysis (jdeps, Structure101) been run, or is all coupling characterization still based on developer intuition and accidental discovery?
What is the dedicated squad size for the 90-day auth independence work? 2-3 engineers with no context-switching is the minimum viable allocation.

declared_losses.why: The 90-day CEO mandate makes payments a secondary concern during the 90-day window. Concurrent auth+payments extraction requires more team capacity than is likely available. Option C is superseded by a cleaner sequencing: auth independence first (90 days), payments extraction second (months 4-6).

declared_losses.what_was_lost: Specific framing of team fear as a planning variable as a standalone concern.
declared_losses.why: Absorbed into Risk and Constraint Model and 90-Day Auth Independence Target strands where it is more actionable. The standalone Team Dynamics strand added overhead without additional load-bearing content given the 90-day mandate now dominates the near-term planning frame.

declared_losses.why: Not relevant to the current 90-day planning and sequencing focus. Recoverable when auth implementation becomes the active focus. Carrying it forward is noise.

Lead with token TTL as the primary lever, then dual-write write path, then shadow-read validation phase, then hard cutover. Redis Cluster specifics if they confirm cloud infrastructure. (b) User reveals session TTL (likely answer: 24h or 30 days — the latter makes the natural drain strategy slower and may require explicit token re-issuance). (c) User reveals infrastructure is on-prem — changes auth DB replacement options significantly; Redis Cluster is still viable but managed PostgreSQL options disappear. (d) User asks how to present the 90-day plan to the CEO — focus on: what the CEO gets at day 90 (testable: take down payments, auth still serves), what is explicitly not in scope, and what the 6-month plan delivers. (e) User reveals the database is a single shared schema — significant update to Database and Schema Separation strand required. (f) User asks about team composition and whether 90 days is realistic given their actual headcount. Watch for: any additional accidental coupling discoveries. If another surprise dependency surfaces before the dependency mapping sprint is done, recommend explicitly pausing all extraction planning until the sprint completes. Two accidental discoveries in a 500K LOC codebase before tooling has been run is a strong signal of deeper entanglement than currently modeled.

strands.title: System Profile
strands.content: 500K LOC Java monolith on Tomcat. Domains: payment processing, inventory management, user auth, order fulfillment. Two binding timeline constraints: 90-day CEO mandate for auth independence from payments, and 6-month full migration window. These are nested — the 90-day target is a milestone within the 6-month plan, not a competing deadline. Limited budget (magnitude still unquantified). PCI-DSS is a hard constraint on payments. Database topology unconfirmed but almost certainly a single shared schema. Session tokens confirmed stored in payments DB. The migration was triggered by a specific incident: payments outage cascaded to auth outage, which took down the entire application. This is the empirical origin of the 90-day mandate and defines the exact failure mode the first phase must solve.

90-day CEO mandate for auth independence from payments is a binding hard constraint.
6-month full migration window with limited budget is a binding constraint.

PCI-DSS applies to the payments domain and cannot be deferred.
Session tokens are stored in the payments DB due to its uptime guarantees.
The payments DB is load-bearing for at least two domains: payments and auth.
Payments module is highly entangled: reaches into inventory, order state, and user profiles.
Database is almost certainly a single shared schema with cross-domain joins.

strands.title: 90-Day Auth Independence Target
strands.content:
New strand introduced cycle 4. The 90-day mandate is for auth independence from payments specifically — not full microservices delivery. Three interpretations were defined and scoped:

(A) Session tokens moved to dedicated store — auth survives payments outage. Achievable in 90 days.
(B) Auth service fully extracted, monolith delegates to it. Achievable in 90 days if auth is the team's singular focus.
(C) Full auth + payments separation with clean PCI boundaries. 6-month work. Not 90-day committable.

Recommended commitment: A + B together. Specific, testable, demoable. Must be defined in writing with the CEO before the sprint starts.

At day 90, the expected state is: auth service running independently, payments outage cannot cascade to auth, session tokens out of payments DB, API gateway in place, circuit breakers between services. What is explicitly NOT delivered by day 90: payments extracted, inventory/orders touched, full PCI boundaries, broader migration completed.

Two CEO expectation risks: (1) CEO mental model may be '90 days = full migration' — must be corrected now, not at day 89. (2) The 90-day target requires the team to be protected from context-switching — CEO mandate implies CEO protection of team focus.

Circuit breaker requirement: the cascading failure was caused by improper bulkheading, not just co-location. Even after auth extraction, a payments degradation can propagate without circuit breakers. Resilience4j (not Hystrix — legacy) must be implemented as part of the 90-day delivery, not as a follow-on.

key_claims.text: 90-day target is achievable if scoped to Interpretations A+B: session tokens migrated, auth service extracted.
Interpretation C (full PCI boundary cleanup) is not achievable in 90 days and must not be committed to.
Circuit breakers (Resilience4j) are a required 90-day deliverable, not an afterthought.
The CEO's mental model of the 90-day target must be confirmed in writing before the sprint starts.
The team must be dedicated (2-3 engineers, no context-switching) for the 90-day target to hold.
Skipping the 2-week dependency mapping sprint to save time is the most likely way to blow the 90-day deadline.

strands.title: Auth-Payments DB Entanglement
strands.content:
Empirical finding (cycle 3, user-stated): session tokens live in the payments DB because that DB carries the highest uptime SLA. This creates a hard coupling between auth extraction and the payments data layer — the two extractions are not independent.

Four specific implications:

(1) PCI scope expansion risk: the CDE includes the payments DB. Session tokens there may pull auth session infrastructure into PCI audit scope. Needs QSA confirmation in weeks 1-2.

(2) Sequencing constraint: payments DB cannot be handed to the payment service while it still contains session tokens. Session tokens must migrate out first to a store with equivalent uptime.

(3) Data migration complexity: session tokens are live stateful data. Naive cutover causes mass logout. Zero-downtime migration requires dual-write during transition. Token TTL is a natural drain lever — if sessions expire in 24h, the old store is naturally drained within 24h of cutover.

(4) Uptime SLA preservation: the replacement auth store must meet or exceed the payments DB uptime SLA. Redis Cluster or managed PostgreSQL with multi-AZ replication are candidate solutions. A default single-node auth DB is not acceptable — it would recreate the original availability problem in a new location.

The 90-day mandate makes this the critical path: session token migration must complete before auth service is considered independent. This is not a phase 2 concern.

key_claims.text: Session tokens in the payments DB may expand PCI audit scope to include auth session infrastructure.
The payments DB cannot be handed to the payment service until session tokens are migrated out.
Zero-downtime session token migration requires dual-write during transition.
Token TTL is a migration lever — short-lived tokens drain naturally from the old store without explicit invalidation.
Any replacement session store must match the uptime SLA of the payments DB.

user_message: The team lead just pushed back. She says we should build a new auth service from scratch rather than extracting the existing one. Her argument: the current auth code is coupled to the ORM layer and would need a rewrite anyway. Counter-argument: a greenfield auth service means we're maintaining two auth systems in parallel during migration. What's your recommendation?
generated_response:
This is a real disagreement worth taking seriously. The team lead is not wrong — but I think she's right about the diagnosis and wrong about the prescription.

---

## My Recommendation: Greenfield, But With a Hard Strangler Constraint

Build the new auth service from scratch **only if** you commit upfront to a strict, time-boxed parallel operation window and a hard cutover date. The danger of greenfield isn't the new build — it's the indefinite parallel operation that follows when the cutover gets deprioritized.

Here's the distinction that matters:

**The team lead's implicit assumption:** Greenfield is cleaner therefore faster.
**The actual risk:** Greenfield is cleaner but it decouples delivery from cutover. Extraction forces cutover because the old code is being actively dismantled. Greenfield lets you defer it.

---

## Why She's Right About the Diagnosis

Take the ORM coupling argument seriously. If the auth code is deeply entangled with your ORM layer — shared entity classes, transaction management that bleeds across domain boundaries, Hibernate session handling baked into auth logic — then extraction isn't really extraction. It's a rewrite with extra steps and legacy constraints. You'd be carrying the old coupling into the new service as scaffolding debt.

Specific signals that would confirm her position:
- Auth logic lives in the same transaction scope as payment or order operations
- User entity classes are shared between auth and other domains via ORM inheritance or direct import
- Session management is handled via ORM-managed entities rather than a dedicated token store
- The auth code imports more than 20% of its dependencies from non-auth packages

If any of these are true, she's correct that extraction is a false economy.

---

## Why She's Wrong About the Consequence

Two auth systems in parallel is not just an inconvenience. It's a specific category of risk:

**Security surface doubles during migration.** Every day you run two auth systems is a day where a vulnerability in either one affects your users. For a PCI-DSS environment this is not abstract — it's an audit finding waiting to happen.

**Feature parity drift starts immediately.** The moment the new auth service is live, someone will add a feature to one but not the other. Password reset flow, MFA, session invalidation on password change — these are not simple to keep synchronized across two implementations.

**The 90-day CEO deadline doesn't care about your parallel operation window.** If you start a greenfield build in week 3, you need the new service production-ready, migrated, and the old one decommissioned by day 90. That's approximately 10 weeks of build time with zero slippage tolerance. Extraction of even messy code is typically faster than that because the behavior is already specified — it exists and runs in production.

---

## The Actual Decision Framework

Ask one question: **How much of the existing auth code is salvageable as behavior specification?**

You don't need to reuse the old code. You need to reuse the old behavior as a test oracle.

```
Salvageable (argue for extraction-with-rewrite):
  - The auth rules are clear even if the code is messy
  - Edge cases are encoded in existing behavior you can capture in tests
  - The ORM coupling is in infrastructure, not business logic

Not salvageable (argue for greenfield):
  - Auth rules are inconsistent or wrong and need redesign
  - Security model has known flaws that need architectural changes
  - The coupling is in business logic, not just infrastructure layer
```

If the existing auth behavior is basically correct — it just has ugly implementation coupling — then write characterization tests against the existing system first, build the new service to pass those tests, and call it a rewrite-during-extraction rather than greenfield. This gives you the team lead's clean implementation without the parallel operation risk.

---

## Concrete Proposal to Bring to the Team Lead

```
Week 1-2:  Write characterization tests for existing auth behavior
           (every endpoint, every edge case you can find)
           This work is required regardless of which path you choose.

Week 3:    Decision gate — review ORM coupling in detail
           If coupling is in infrastructure only: extraction path
           If coupling is in business logic: greenfield path
           Either way, the characterization tests are your spec.

Greenfield path (if chosen):
  - New service must be feature-complete by week 8
  - Parallel operation window is fixed at 2 weeks maximum
  - Hard cutover at week 10 with old auth code deletion scheduled
  - No extensions. Treat the deletion date like a production deploy.

Extraction path (if chosen):
  - Strip ORM coupling first, within the monolith, before extraction
  - Extract clean code, not coupled code
  - Faster to production, lower parallel operation risk
```

The key concession to the team lead: if you go greenfield, you go greenfield properly. Parallel operation is not open-ended. The old auth system gets a deletion date in the calendar before the new one is written.

---

## What I'd Actually Say to Her

"You're right that we shouldn't extract coupled code and call it a microservice. If the ORM entanglement is in the business logic, not just the infrastructure, build it clean. But I need a hard commitment: the old auth system gets a death date before we write the first line of the new one. And we write characterization tests this week so we know exactly what we're rebuilding. If we can't agree on a deletion date, we default to extraction because at least extraction forces the cutover."

That's a reasonable position that respects her technical judgment while protecting against the indefinite parallel operation failure mode.

---

Two questions that would resolve this quickly: What does the ORM coupling actually look like — is auth logic inside JPA entity methods or Hibernate event listeners, or is it just that auth uses the same EntityManager infrastructure? And does the existing auth system have any test coverage at all, or are we flying blind on behavior specification?

strands.title: System Profile
strands.content: 500K LOC Java monolith on Tomcat. Four domains: payments, inventory, user auth, order fulfillment. 6-month migration timeline, limited unquantified budget, PCI-DSS hard constraint. Coupling profile is materially worse than initial characterization: payments reaches into inventory, order state, and user profiles (cycle 2), payments DB is confirmed to store auth session tokens (cycle 3, empirical), and payments outage cascaded to auth and caused full application outage last quarter (cycle 4, empirical). That outage is the confirmed trigger for this migration. CEO has issued a hard 90-day constraint for auth independence from payments. This adds a second timeline: 90-day auth independence target running inside the 6-month full migration. Infrastructure details (cloud vs. on-prem, session token TTL) remain unconfirmed — these were explicitly solicited at end of cycle 4 and are critical path inputs for the dual-write migration plan.

CEO has mandated auth independence from payments within 90 days — hard external constraint
Session tokens are stored in the payments DB due to its uptime guarantees — empirical, user-stated cycle 3
Payments DB is multi-tenant: carries CDE payment data and auth session state simultaneously
Infrastructure (cloud vs. on-prem) and session token TTL are unconfirmed — explicitly solicited

strands.title: 90-Day Auth Independence Target
strands.content: New strand — CEO-mandated hard constraint from cycle 4. The 90-day target is achievable but only under specific scoping and resourcing conditions. Correct interpretation: auth service extracted, session tokens on dedicated HA store, payments outage cannot cascade to auth — this is testable and demoable. Wrong interpretation: full auth + payments separation with clean PCI boundaries — that is 6-month work. The CEO definition must be locked in writing within the first two weeks or the target will be evaluated against the wrong scope at day 89. Proposed 90-day sequence: Weeks 1-2 dependency mapping + QSA emergency engagement + auth DB provisioning; Weeks 3-6 session token dual-write migration; Weeks 7-10 auth service extraction proper + Resilience4j circuit breakers; Weeks 11-12 cutover, chaos testing, validation. Critical failure modes for the 90-day target: (1) dependency mapping skipped to save time — this is how mid-extraction surprises blow the timeline; (2) team splits focus between auth extraction and other work — requires dedicated 2-3 engineer squad protected from context-switching; (3) auth DB uptime SLA not resolved in week 1 — the uptime rationale for payments DB must be matched or the problem is just relocated; (4) QSA engagement becomes a blocker — must be framed as scoping confirmation (2-week turnaround), not a full assessment. Circuit breaker work (Resilience4j) is part of the 90-day delivery, not an afterthought — the cascade failure was a bulkheading problem, not only a co-location problem.
depends_on: Auth Service Design and Session Migration

key_claims.text: 90-day auth independence is achievable if scoped to: auth service extracted, session tokens on dedicated HA store, payments outage cannot cascade to auth
90-day scope must be defined in writing with CEO within first 2 weeks — wrong scope definition is a primary failure mode
Cascade failure was a bulkheading problem, not only a co-location problem — circuit breakers required even after extraction
Resilience4j is the correct circuit breaker library for this Java ecosystem — Hystrix is legacy
Dedicated 2-3 engineer squad protected from context-switching is required to hit 90 days
At day 90, payments extraction and PCI boundary cleanup will not be complete — CEO mental model must match this

strands.title: Extraction Sequencing Strategy
strands.content: The 90-day CEO constraint resolves the cycle 3 sequencing ambiguity in favor of auth-first extraction, but with a critical reframe: auth independence from payments is the deliverable, not auth extraction in isolation. This collapses Options A/B/C from cycle 3 into a single mandated path — auth must move first and fast. The coordinated dual extraction (Option C) remains structurally correct but the gap period is now time-bounded by the 90-day target, not open-ended. Payments extraction begins after auth independence is confirmed — this is now the explicit sequencing: auth independence by day 90, then payments extraction in the remaining ~3 months of the 6-month window. Inventory and order fulfillment remain deferred to phase 2 as previously established. The internal OrderStateUpdate API seam for payments→order state write dependency remains the interim pattern. All previously invalidated week-by-week timelines remain invalidated; the 90-day sequence in the new strand replaces them for the auth phase. Payments phase timeline requires reconstruction after auth work is underway and dependency mapping results are available. Strangler fig pattern still governs all extractions.

key_claims.text: 90-day CEO mandate resolves cycle 3 sequencing ambiguity — auth independence from payments is now the mandated first deliverable
Payments extraction begins after auth independence is confirmed — explicit sequencing, not assumption
Payments extraction fits in remaining ~3 months after 90-day auth target, but this is tight given coupling complexity
Fear-driven deferral of payments is an anti-pattern with compounding cost
All extraction timelines are invalidated pending dependency mapping and QSA input

strands.title: Coupling and Dependency Analysis
strands.content: Coupling profile has worsened each cycle through discovery, not revision of priors. Cycle 1: generic assumption of moderate coupling. Cycle 2: payments reaches into inventory (query), order state (write — hardest tentacle), and user profiles (read). Cycle 3: auth session tokens live in payments DB — cyclic data-layer dependency. Cycle 4: payments outage cascade to auth confirmed empirically — the coupling is not just architectural, it has a proven operational blast radius. The cascade failure validates the coupling analysis and makes it a business-language argument, not just a technical one. Static analysis tooling (jdeps, Structure101) remains a hard prerequisite — the session token discovery was accidental, and the confirmed cascade failure suggests the coupling is deeper than the team's mental model. Coupling tentacles catalogued: (1) Payments→Inventory: stock validation query, synchronous, distributed transaction risk. (2) Payments→Order state: write dependency, hardest tentacle, event-driven boundary or internal API callback seam required. (3) Payments→User profiles: billing data read, easiest, resolvable via read API. (4) Auth↔Payments DB: session tokens in payments DB, bidirectional operational dependency, confirmed blast radius.

key_claims.text: Payments outage cascade to full application outage is empirically confirmed — coupling has proven operational blast radius
Auth session tokens in payments DB creates a cyclic data-layer dependency between auth and payments extractions
Pattern of accidental coupling discovery means team intuition cannot be trusted as the dependency map
Payments write dependency on order state is the hardest extraction tentacle — requires event-driven boundary or callback seam
jdeps or Structure101 analysis is a hard prerequisite for extraction work

strands.title: Payment Service Design and PCI-DSS
strands.content: Third-party card vault (Stripe/Braintree/Adyen) for scope reduction remains the primary architectural driver. QSA engagement is now a week 1-2 requirement — framed as emergency scoping confirmation, not a full assessment, with an explicit 2-week turnaround expectation. The session tokens in CDE is a live compliance question and a potential audit surface problem that predates the migration. Migration framing for QSA: this is a compliance improvement — moving session tokens out of the CDE, reducing audit surface, isolating payment data. The payments DB cannot be handed to the payment service until session tokens are migrated out — hard sequencing constraint. Network segmentation required from initial deployment. Payment Service API boundary: initiatePayment(OrderId, PaymentToken, Amount), refund(TransactionId, Amount), getStatus(TransactionId). Internal OrderStateUpdate endpoint on monolith remains the interim pattern for order state write dependency. Payments extraction is explicitly post-90-day-auth-target work — this gives the team ~3 months after auth independence to execute payments extraction, which is tight given coupling complexity.

key_claims.text: Third-party card vault dramatically reduces PCI-DSS scope — system should never handle raw PANs
QSA must be engaged at week 1-2 as emergency scoping confirmation with 2-week turnaround expectation
Payments DB cannot be handed to payment service until session tokens are migrated out — hard sequencing constraint
Network segmentation for payment service is required from initial deployment

strands.title: Auth Service Design and Session Migration
strands.content: Auth service extraction is now the primary 90-day deliverable. Design parameters from prior cycles preserved: Keycloak or Spring Authorization Server, JWT with 15-minute access tokens, longer refresh tokens, monolith delegates during transition. Session token migration is the critical path item and the first execution risk. Key constraints: (1) Uptime — auth service DB must meet or exceed the payments DB SLA. This is not optional; the original placement of tokens in payments DB was explicitly uptime-motivated. Managed HA DB required: RDS Multi-AZ, Cloud Spanner, or Redis Cluster with replication. The specific infrastructure choice depends on whether the team is on cloud or on-prem — this is an open question explicitly solicited in cycle 4. (2) Zero-downtime migration via dual-write: write tokens to both payments DB and new auth DB simultaneously, validate reads from new store in shadow mode, drain old store passively using token TTL, cut reads over only after validation. Short token TTLs are a migration lever — 15-minute access tokens drain naturally within one TTL window after dual-write is established. (3) Scope of auth artifacts in payments DB is still unconfirmed — session tokens may not be the only auth data present. Must confirm before migration scope is fixed. (4) Circuit breakers (Resilience4j) must be in place before or during cutover — the outage was a cascade failure, not just a co-location problem. Bulkheads are part of the auth independence deliverable.

Session token migration requires dual-write strategy to avoid mass user logout
Short JWT access token TTL (15 min) is a migration lever — natural drain from old store within one TTL window
Use Keycloak or Spring Authorization Server — do not build custom token management
Scope of auth artifacts in payments DB is unknown — may extend beyond session tokens
Circuit breakers are part of the auth independence deliverable, not post-migration polish

strands.title: Risk and Constraint Management
strands.content: Risk profile has a new confirmed data point: the cascade failure is empirical, not theoretical. This changes the risk register from speculative to evidenced. Primary risk vectors: (1) Hidden coupling — session token discovery was accidental; cascade failure confirms coupling has real operational blast radius. Dependency mapping is a hard prerequisite. (2) PCI scope creep — session tokens in CDE is live. (3) Timeline compression — 90-day target is tight; two timelines now running (90-day auth independence + 6-month full migration). (4) Uptime regression — auth DB must match payments DB SLA or the problem is relocated, not solved. Technical debt inventory: shared DB for inventory/orders (named, intentional, phase 1), OrderStateUpdate internal API seam (named, intentional, retirement condition: orders extracted), gap period where monolith reads session tokens from payments DB during dual-write phase (named, time-bounded to weeks 3-6). strands.title: System Profile
strands.content: 500K LOC Java monolith on Tomcat. Four domains: payments, inventory, user auth, order fulfillment. 6-month migration timeline, limited unquantified budget, PCI-DSS hard constraint. Coupling profile is materially worse than initial characterization: payments reaches into inventory, order state, and user profiles (cycle 2), payments DB is confirmed to store auth session tokens (cycle 3, empirical), and payments outage cascaded to auth and caused full application outage last quarter (cycle 4, empirical). That outage is the confirmed trigger for this migration. CEO has issued a hard 90-day constraint for auth independence from payments. Infrastructure details (cloud vs. on-prem, session token TTL) remain unconfirmed — explicitly solicited. Auth ORM coupling nature is now an open question with direct bearing on build-vs-extract decision.

strands.title: Auth Service Design and Build Strategy
strands.content:
New strand integrating cycle 5 greenfield-vs-extraction debate with prior auth design work. Team lead argues for greenfield auth service on grounds that auth code is ORM-coupled and needs a rewrite anyway. Counter-position accepted in part: if ORM coupling is in business logic (auth rules inside JPA entity methods, Hibernate event listeners), greenfield is justified. If coupling is in infrastructure only (shared EntityManager, connection pool), extraction-with-refactor is faster and safer. The diagnostic question is: where does the coupling live?

Recommendation: greenfield is acceptable only under a hard strangler constraint — deletion date for old auth system must be set before new service is written. Two-week maximum parallel operation window. Without this commitment, greenfield becomes indefinite parallel operation, which doubles security surface and creates feature parity drift. Characterization tests against existing auth behavior are required regardless of path — they are the behavior specification for the new service and the test oracle during cutover.

Prior design parameters preserved: Keycloak or Spring Authorization Server, JWT with 15-minute access tokens, longer refresh tokens, monolith delegates during transition. Session token migration (dual-write, shadow-read, TTL drain) applies to both paths — the migration mechanics are independent of whether the new service is extracted or greenfield. Resilience4j circuit breakers remain part of the 90-day delivery.

Decision gate proposed for week 3: after characterization tests are written and ORM coupling is inspected in detail, choose path formally. This prevents premature commitment while maintaining momentum.
depends_on: Auth-Payments DB Entanglement and Session Migration

key_claims.text: Greenfield auth service is acceptable if ORM coupling is in business logic — but requires hard deletion date for old system before build begins
Indefinite parallel operation of two auth systems doubles security surface and creates feature parity drift — primary failure mode of ungoverned greenfield
Characterization tests against existing auth behavior are required regardless of build path
ORM coupling location (business logic vs. infrastructure) is the deciding diagnostic — unconfirmed
Extraction-with-rewrite is faster to cutover than greenfield because behavior is pre-specified by production system
Use Keycloak or Spring Authorization Server — do not build custom token management
Two-week maximum parallel operation window must be enforced if greenfield path is chosen

strands.title: Auth-Payments DB Entanglement and Session Migration
strands.content:
Consolidation of prior Auth-Payments DB Entanglement strand with session migration mechanics. Session tokens in payments DB is empirically confirmed. Creates cyclic extraction dependency: auth extraction needs payments DB access resolved; payments extraction complicated by auth data in its DB. Payments DB is confirmed multi-tenant: carries CDE payment data and auth session state simultaneously — a live PCI scope problem.

Session token migration is critical path regardless of whether auth service is extracted or greenfield. Dual-write strategy: write tokens to both payments DB and new auth DB simultaneously, validate reads from new store in shadow mode, drain old store passively via token TTL expiry, cut reads over only after validation. Short token TTLs (15-minute access tokens if JWT) are a migration lever — natural drain within one TTL window. Token TTL is unconfirmed and explicitly solicited.

Auth DB replacement must match or exceed payments DB uptime SLA — the uptime rationale for original placement is a system requirement, not a historical accident. Managed HA required: RDS Multi-AZ, Cloud Spanner, or Redis Cluster depending on infrastructure (unconfirmed). Payments DB cannot be handed to payment service until session tokens are fully migrated out — hard sequencing constraint that is independent of build-vs-extract decision.

Scope of auth artifacts in payments DB is still unconfirmed — may extend beyond session tokens to credentials, API keys, CSRF tokens.

key_claims.text: Session tokens in payments DB is empirically confirmed — creates hard data-layer dependency between auth and payments extractions
Auth DB must match payments DB uptime SLA — single-instance replacement is not acceptable
Dual-write session token migration is required to avoid mass user logout during cutover
Payments DB cannot be handed to payment service until session tokens are migrated out — hard sequencing constraint
Scope of auth artifacts in payments DB may extend beyond session tokens — unconfirmed

strands.title: 90-Day Auth Independence Target
strands.content:
CEO-mandated hard constraint. 90-day target is achievable but requires precise scoping, dedicated resourcing, and no timeline slippage on dependency mapping. Correct interpretation: auth service extracted or greenfield-built, session tokens on dedicated HA store, payments outage cannot cascade to auth — testable and demoable. Wrong interpretation: full auth + payments separation with clean PCI boundaries — that is 6-month work.

Greenfield debate (cycle 5) does not change the 90-day feasibility assessment materially, but it does affect risk profile: greenfield adds build time and parallel operation risk that extraction avoids. If greenfield path is chosen, the deletion date for old auth must be inside the 90-day window.

Proposed sequence (revised to reflect build-path decision gate): Weeks 1-2 dependency mapping + characterization tests + QSA engagement + auth DB provisioning; Week 3 decision gate on greenfield vs. extraction; Weeks 3-6 session token dual-write migration; Weeks 7-10 auth service build/extraction + Resilience4j circuit breakers; Weeks 11-12 cutover, chaos testing, old auth deletion, validation.

Critical failure modes: (1) ORM coupling inspection skipped — wrong build path chosen without evidence; (2) parallel operation window left open-ended if greenfield chosen; (3) dependency mapping compressed to save time; (4) team splits focus — requires dedicated 2-3 engineer squad; (5) auth DB uptime SLA not resolved week 1; (6) CEO scope mismatch not corrected early.
depends_on: Auth Service Design and Build Strategy
Auth-Payments DB Entanglement and Session Migration

Greenfield path is compatible with 90-day target only if old auth system deletion date is inside the window
90-day scope must be defined in writing with CEO within first 2 weeks
Cascade failure was a bulkheading problem — Resilience4j circuit breakers are part of the 90-day deliverable
At day 90, payments extraction and full PCI boundary cleanup will not be complete

strands.title: Payment Service Design and PCI-DSS

QSA engagement is now a week 1-2 requirement — framed as emergency scoping confirmation, not full assessment. Session tokens in CDE is a live compliance question predating the migration. Migration framing for QSA: compliance improvement, not risk event.

Payments DB cannot be handed to payment service until session tokens are migrated out — hard sequencing constraint. Network segmentation required from initial deployment. Payment Service API boundary: initiatePayment(OrderId, PaymentToken, Amount), refund(TransactionId, Amount), getStatus(TransactionId). Internal OrderStateUpdate endpoint on monolith remains interim pattern for order state write dependency. Payments extraction is post-90-day-auth-target work.

Payments DB cannot be handed to payment service until session tokens are migrated out

Pattern of accidental discovery in a 500K LOC codebase means the team's mental model cannot be trusted as the dependency map. jdeps or Structure101 analysis remains a hard prerequisite. Characterization tests for auth (cycle 5 recommendation) serve double duty: behavior specification and dependency surface revelation.
